items.title: Lowe's Q1 FY26 — adj EPS $3.03 beats $2.97 est, revenue $23.1B tops $22.97B est; FY26 EPS guide affirmed at $12.25–$12.75 but LOW slips ~2% premarket on guide midpoint below consensus
items.summary: Lowe's posts EPS and revenue beat with comp sales +0.6% and online +15.5%, but FY26 EPS midpoint of $12.50 sits below ~$12.59 consensus, sending shares about 2% lower premarket.

explainer.tagline: Lowe's clears the Q1 bar but cautious FY26 guide overshadows the beat.
explainer.whatIsIt: Lowe's reported first-quarter fiscal 2026 sales and earnings on May 20, 2026, posting net earnings of $1.6 billion. Adjusted diluted EPS rose 3.8% to $3.03, while GAAP diluted EPS was $2.90 after $96M of pre-tax acquisition costs. Total sales hit $23.1 billion versus $20.9 billion a year ago.
explainer.howItWorks: Comparable sales rose 0.6%, driven by strong spring execution and 15.5% online sales growth. The company affirmed FY26 guidance: total sales of $92.0–94.0 billion (+7–9% Y/Y), comps flat to +2%, adjusted diluted EPS of $12.25–$12.75, and operating margin of 11.2–11.4%. Acquisition-related costs of $96M came out of the GAAP number.
explainer.whyItMatters: The print extends a divergent home-improvement read: HD slipped on a similar beat yesterday, and LOW followed with shares ~2% lower premarket as the FY26 EPS midpoint of $12.50 came in roughly a dime below ~$12.59 consensus. Online momentum and appliances are offsetting tepid Pro and big-ticket discretionary demand. Read-through is mixed for housing-tied names across the supply chain.

image.alt: Lowe's Companies corporate logo

items.title: Analog Devices FQ2 2026 — record revenue $3.62B (+37% Y/Y), adj EPS $3.09 trounces $2.90 est; Q3 guide $3.9B with adj EPS $3.30 ±$0.15, but ADI slips ~2% premarket as semis hide from euphoria
items.summary: ADI delivered record FQ2 revenue $3.62B (+37%) with adj EPS $3.09, both above the high end of its outlook. Q3 guide of $3.9B revenue and adj EPS $3.30 was strong, but ADI traded ~2% lower premarket.

explainer.tagline: ADI prints a record analog quarter — and the stock fades on it.
explainer.whatIsIt: Analog Devices reported fiscal Q2 2026 results on May 20, 2026 at 7am ET. Revenue of $3.623 billion (+37% Y/Y) and adjusted diluted EPS of $3.09 both topped the high end of company guidance. GAAP diluted EPS came in at $2.40. The dividend was lifted to $1.10/share.
explainer.howItWorks: CEO Vincent Roche flagged record bookings across Industrial, Automotive and Communications segments. Trailing-twelve-month operating cash flow reached $5.1 billion or 40% of revenue. ADI returned $1.3B to shareholders in Q2 via dividends and buybacks. Q3 guidance: revenue $3.9B ±$100M, reported op margin ~39%, adjusted op margin ~49%, adjusted EPS $3.30 ±$0.15.
explainer.whyItMatters: The print confirms the broader analog cycle rebound but came against an over-positioned semis tape — options had priced a ~10% move. With ADI down ~2% premarket despite a beat-and-raise, the read-through for Texas Instruments and NXP is that fundamentals are real but sentiment is fragile heading into NVDA's after-bell earnings.

image.alt: Analog Devices corporate logo

items.title: CAVA Q1 2026 — revenue $438M (+32% Y/Y) beats, comp sales +9.7% on +6.8% traffic, EPS $0.20 vs $0.18 est; raises FY26 adj EBITDA guide to $181M–$191M; CAVA +6% AH
items.summary: CAVA delivered a clean Q1 beat with same-restaurant sales +9.7% (traffic +6.8%) and 25.1% restaurant-level margin, raising FY26 same-restaurant-sales guide to 4.5–6.5% and lifting adj EBITDA outlook.

explainer.tagline: CAVA prints a 9.7% comp and the market crowns the next Chipotle — for now.
explainer.whatIsIt: CAVA Group reported Q1 fiscal 2026 results on May 19, 2026 after the close. Revenue rose 32.2% to $438.3 million, EPS of $0.20 beat $0.18 estimates, and adjusted EBITDA grew 37.6% to $61.7 million.
explainer.howItWorks: Same-restaurant sales jumped 9.7%, including 6.8% traffic growth. The company opened 20 net new restaurants, bringing the total to 459. Restaurant-level profit margin held at 25.1%. CAVA raised FY26 guidance across the board: same-restaurant-sales now seen at +4.5–6.5% (vs +3.0–5.0% prior), net new openings 75–77 (vs 74–76), and adjusted EBITDA $181–191M (vs $176–184M).
explainer.whyItMatters: The comp print is the standout — 9.7% with positive traffic in a quarter where peers from Wendy's to Shake Shack have flagged consumer weakness. The Mediterranean fast-casual store-base is still small (459 vs Chipotle's 3,700+), giving runway, and shares gained ~6% after-hours.

image.alt: CAVA Group Q1 2026 earnings announcement card

items.title: US 30-year Treasury yield tops 5.197% on May 19 — highest since July 2007; S&P 500 -0.67% on inflation-from-Iran fears
items.summary: Long-bond yield briefly hits 5.197% — a 19-year high — as traders dump duration on Iran-war oil shock; 10Y to 4.687%, S&P -0.67% on third straight losing day. 20Y auction Wednesday.

explainer.tagline: Long-end Treasuries break to a 19-year high as the Iran-war oil shock seeps into the inflation outlook.
explainer.whatIsIt: The 30-year US Treasury yield briefly hit 5.197% on Tuesday, May 19 — its highest level since July 2007, before the global financial crisis. The 10-year yield touched 4.687%, the most since January 2025. Equity indexes broke lower with the S&P 500 closing -0.67% at 7,353.61, its third straight losing session.
explainer.howItWorks: Yields rose six basis points intraday on the long end as traders dumped duration over Iran-war energy spillovers and a wider fiscal deficit. With the Strait of Hormuz still effectively closed, oil and gas prices are at four-year highs, and that is now showing up in food and airfares. The 20-year auction is scheduled for Wednesday, May 20, giving the market another supply test at the long end.
explainer.whyItMatters: Higher long yields raise mortgage, credit-card, and corporate borrowing costs and pressure equity multiples; rate-sensitive groups (homebuilders, REITs, regional banks, megacap tech) carry the biggest mark-down risk on each fresh leg higher. Citi flagged 5.5% as the next focus level if the selloff extends. Tomorrow's NVDA print after the close becomes a key swing factor for whether the AI bid can absorb the rate move.
explainer.forWho: rates traders, allocators, equity duration desks

image.alt: Trader on the floor as Treasury yields hit a 19-year high

items.title: Astera Labs +~15% on May 19 as Evercore lifts PT to $297 (from $215); CEO touts X-Series AI fabric switch at JPMorgan TMT conference
items.summary: ALAB jumps ~15% to ~$248 — Evercore ISI's $297 target becomes Wall Street's highest as CEO Jitendra Mohan showcases Scorpio X-Series 320-lane PCIe-6 AI fabric at JPM TMT, citing AI-inference ramp into late 2026.

explainer.tagline: Evercore makes ALAB Wall Street's highest target and the CEO talks up PCIe-6 AI fabric — shares run ~15%.
explainer.whatIsIt: Astera Labs jumped roughly 15% to about $248.36 on Tuesday, May 19, putting the stock within sight of its September 2025 record near $263. Catalyst was twofold — Evercore ISI raised its price target to $297 (from $215, keeping Outperform), now the highest published target on the Street, and CEO Jitendra Mohan presented the company's PCIe-6 Scorpio X-Series 320-lane AI fabric switch at JPMorgan's Global TMT Conference.
explainer.howItWorks: Evercore's check work points to AI-inference workloads taking center stage by late 2026, expanding the addressable market for ALAB's connectivity portfolio (signal conditioners, PCIe switches, memory controllers, cable modules). The company already reported Q1 FY26 revenue of $308.4M (+93% Y/Y) on May 5, well ahead of the $292.2M consensus. The stock has now rallied roughly 150% from its early-April low.
explainer.whyItMatters: ALAB has emerged as a key tell on the AI scale-up vs scale-out transition: a higher PT and a high-profile CEO appearance frame the next leg of hyperscaler capex as bandwidth-bound, not just GPU-bound. Read-through is constructive for the broader AI connectivity complex (AVGO, MRVL, CRDO) and for NVDA's Wednesday-night print, where management commentary on networking/Spectrum-X uptake will be closely parsed.

image.alt: Rising stock chart illustrating Astera Labs' May 19 rally

items.title: Home Depot Q1 FY26 — net sales $41.8B (+4.8% Y/Y) tops $41.5B est, adj EPS $3.43 beats $3.41; comps +0.6%, FY26 guide reaffirmed; HD ~-1% premarket
items.summary: Home Depot delivered Q1 FY26 net sales of $41.8B (+4.8% Y/Y) on +0.6% total comps (+0.4% US), beating consensus. Adj diluted EPS $3.43 vs $3.41 est but down from $3.56 a year ago. FY26 guide reaffirmed.

explainer.tagline: Home Depot Q1 FY26: sales $41.8B (+4.8%) and adj EPS $3.43 narrowly clear consensus; comps barely positive at +0.6%, FY guide untouched.
explainer.whatIsIt: The Home Depot reported its fiscal Q1 2026 (quarter ended May 3, 2026) on the morning of May 19, 2026. Net sales rose 4.8% Y/Y to $41.8 billion while total comparable sales eked out +0.6% (U.S. +0.4%). Both top line and adjusted EPS landed slightly above Street consensus, though profitability fell versus a year ago.
explainer.howItWorks: GAAP diluted EPS came in at $3.30 vs $3.45 a year earlier; adjusted diluted EPS was $3.43 vs $3.56 Y/Y and ~$3.41 consensus. Customer transactions slipped to 391.1M (-0.9%) but average ticket rose 2.3% to $92.76 — growth was ticket-driven, not traffic-driven. Operating income fell ~3% to $4.98B as costs outran the modest top-line lift. Management reaffirmed FY26 guidance: total sales growth ~2.5%–4.5%, comps flat to +2%, ~33.1% gross margin, ~12.8%–13.0% adjusted op margin, and adjusted diluted EPS flat to +4.0% off the FY25 base.
explainer.whyItMatters: HD is the largest U.S. home-improvement retailer and a key read on rate-sensitive housing demand; reaffirming FY guidance after April CPI/PPI hot prints and 10Y yields touching 4.60% argues the housing-adjacent consumer is holding up better than feared. With shares dipping ~1% premarket on a low-quality beat, peers LOW, FND and TTC will trade on the same playbook. Watch the call for pro-vs-DIY mix and Pacific-region read after the Iran-driven energy spike.

items.title: Trump postpones Tuesday Iran strike at Gulf allies' request, citing 'serious negotiations' — Brent slips below $110 from $112 high; June WTI settled +3% Monday at $108.66 before pulling back
items.summary: Trump said on Truth Social Monday afternoon he postponed a 'scheduled attack' on Iran for Tuesday after Saudi Arabia, UAE and Qatar leaders requested time for 'serious negotiations' on a nuclear-free deal. Brent pulled back from $112 toward $109; Pentagon held on standby.

explainer.tagline: Trump backs off planned Tuesday strike on Iran after Saudi/UAE/Qatar lobbied for a negotiating window — oil retreats from $112 Brent.
explainer.whatIsIt: Monday afternoon (May 18) U.S. President Donald Trump posted on Truth Social that he had postponed a 'scheduled attack of Iran tomorrow' after the leaders of Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE personally requested time to allow 'serious negotiations' that they believe will produce a deal acceptable to the U.S. — including the condition that Iran does not get nuclear weapons. Trump said the Pentagon remains on standby to 'go forward with a full, large-scale assault' at a moment's notice if no deal lands.
explainer.howItWorks: Headed into Monday, Brent had pushed to ~$112.10 (+~2% on the session) and June WTI settled +~3% at $108.66 as traders priced in the Tuesday strike risk that Trump had publicly teased over the weekend. Within minutes of the post, Brent shed more than $2 toward $109; by Tuesday morning Brent traded below $110 (-1.8% on the day) and U.S. stock futures pulled back from the prior session's tech selloff. The postponement does not lift sanctions or reopen Hormuz — flows remain disrupted — but it removes the near-term kinetic-strike premium.
explainer.whyItMatters: Oil is the macro variable still feeding April's hot CPI (+3.8% Y/Y) and PPI (+6.0% Y/Y) prints, and Brent above $110 has been the bond market's go-to inflation worry. A genuine off-ramp would let the 10Y back off its 4.60% one-year high; a breakdown in talks puts the strike option back on the table and re-prices the curve in the other direction. For energy desks (XOM, CVX, OXY, MPC) Trump's 'moment's notice' caveat keeps a binary tail on the next 1–2 weeks.

image.alt: CBS News graphic on U.S.-Iran tensions as Trump postpones planned strike

items.title: Seagate -6.9% drags memory complex on May 18 — CEO Mosley says new factories 'would take too long' at JPMorgan TMT; Micron -5.95%, SanDisk and WDC also lower as AI capacity fears bite
items.summary: Seagate fell ~6.9% Monday — its worst session in ~2 months — after CEO Dave Mosley said at JPMorgan TMT that building new factories 'would take too long' and could leave excess capacity. Micron -5.95%, dragging the memory/storage cohort.

metrics.Catalyst: Seagate CEO at JPMorgan TMT — no new fabs

explainer.tagline: Seagate CEO's 'too long to build new factories' line at JPMorgan TMT shears 6.9% off STX and drags memory peers Monday.
explainer.whatIsIt: At the JPMorgan Annual Global Technology, Media and Communications Conference on Monday May 18, Seagate CEO Dave Mosley said building new HDD/storage factories 'would take too long' and risk leaving Seagate with more capacity than it needs. He framed mid-20s % CAGR through technology transitions — pushing per-platter density from 3TB to 4 and 5TB — as the path, not new fabs. STX closed -6.9%, its worst day in roughly two months. The comments amplified an already nervous trade in AI memory: Micron settled at $681.54, -5.95%, and SanDisk and Western Digital also fell.
explainer.howItWorks: Mosley said recording-head wafer lead times already exceed nine months with another quarter to convert into finished drives, so a greenfield fab would not be online in time to matter for the current AI demand cycle. Investor takeaway flipped from 'incumbent operating leverage' to 'incumbents are admitting they can't add supply' — which on the wrong day reads as topped-out volumes rather than tight pricing. The Nasdaq closed -0.51% at 26,090.73 and the S&P 500 -0.07% at 7,403.05; the Dow was up +0.32% as money rotated out of AI-memory leaders.
explainer.whyItMatters: Memory chips and HDDs have been the second leg of the AI capex trade after compute, and DRAM/HBM/NAND have outrun fundamentals into May. Mosley's comments give bears a quotable reason to argue the cycle has peaked structurally even if pricing stays firm. Confirms the May 12 DRAM ETF flush wasn't a one-off — and sets up Nvidia's Wednesday print as the next gate for the entire AI infrastructure thesis.

image.alt: Seagate hard-disk drive manufacturing facility

items.title: Blackstone and Google launch new TPU cloud JV — $5B initial Blackstone equity, first 500MW online in 2027; total commitment can reach ~$25B with leverage; Benjamin Treynor Sloss to lead
items.summary: Blackstone committed $5B initial equity to a new U.S. JV with Google that will sell Google TPU compute as cloud capacity. Blackstone takes majority equity; Google supplies TPUs/software; targets 500MW online in 2027 and up to ~$25B total with leverage. Benjamin Treynor Sloss runs it.

explainer.tagline: Blackstone-led $5B JV with Google will resell TPU compute as a third-party cloud service, with first 500MW online in 2027.
explainer.whatIsIt: Blackstone and Alphabet's Google announced on Monday May 18 a new U.S. joint venture that will provide Google TPU compute as a third-party cloud service, sitting alongside Google Cloud's own TPU offering. Blackstone is committing an initial $5 billion of equity from its funds for a majority stake; Google contributes TPU hardware, software, networking and technical know-how. Benjamin Treynor Sloss — most recently Google's chief programs officer and a 20-year Google infrastructure veteran — has been named CEO. The JV plans to bring its first 500 megawatts of data-center capacity online in 2027.
explainer.howItWorks: Per Blackstone's release, the company is committing 'an initial $5 billion of equity capital' from Blackstone-managed funds. Per Bloomberg, total invested capital could eventually reach roughly $25 billion once debt financing layers on top — making this one of the largest equity-led private-AI-infrastructure vehicles to date. Per WSJ, Blackstone holds majority economic ownership of the new entity. The structure is identical in shape to Blackstone's QTS data-center playbook: lease land, sign offtake, finance with private credit, scale.
explainer.whyItMatters: This is the second multi-billion private-infrastructure vehicle from a megacap in two weeks (after the AVGO/Apollo+Blackstone $35B private-credit rumor for chip build). It signals two things: (1) hyperscalers want compute capacity off-balance-sheet given the magnitude of AI capex, and (2) Google specifically wants to monetize TPU outside its own cloud — a direct shot at Nvidia GPU economics. BX and GOOGL traded up premarket on the print; data-center landlords (EQIX, DLR) and AI-power names (CEG, VST) trade through this announcement.

image.alt: Blackstone corporate logo

items.title: Publicis to acquire LiveRamp in $2.5B all-cash deal — $38.50/share (≈30% premium); RAMP +27%
items.summary: Publicis Groupe will buy LiveRamp for $38.50/share in cash — $2.5B equity value, $2.167B enterprise value, ~30% premium to the May 15 close. RAMP gapped +27% on the May 18 open.

explainer.tagline: Publicis takes LiveRamp private at $38.50/share cash — $2.5B equity, 30% premium, RAMP +27% on the open.
explainer.whatIsIt: Publicis Groupe announced an agreement on May 17, 2026 to acquire ad-tech data-collaboration platform LiveRamp Holdings in an all-cash merger. The price is $38.50 per LiveRamp share, valuing equity at roughly $2.5B and enterprise value at $2.167B after backing out $379M of net cash. Both boards unanimously approved.
explainer.howItWorks: Cash consideration funded with cash on hand plus debt. The price is a ~30% premium to LiveRamp's May 15 close. Reciprocal break fee is $32.35M. Closing is targeted by year-end 2026 subject to LiveRamp shareholder vote and U.S. and non-U.S. antitrust + CFIUS clearance; outside date is May 16, 2027 with a possible three-month extension. Publicis says the deal will be accretive to headline EPS from year one of consolidation and raised 2027–28 organic guidance to +7–8% net revenue and +8–10% headline EPS growth.
explainer.whyItMatters: Largest ad-tech deal in years and the second major identity/data-platform buyout in the cycle — Publicis is folding RAMP's RampID identity graph into its CoreAI 'agentic transformation' stack. RAMP gapped to $37.59 (+27%) on May 18 to sit just below the deal price, leaving roughly 2.4% spread for arbs. Comp ad-tech names (TTD, MGNI, PUBM) will be screened as next-leg consolidation targets.

image.alt: LiveRamp and Publicis Groupe press-release graphic announcing the $2.5B all-cash acquisition

items.title: Japan 30Y JGB hits record 4.17%, 10Y JGB at 2.8% 29-yr high as Takaichi orders supplementary budget; US 10Y touches 4.63% 15-month high
items.summary: Global bond rout: Japan's 30Y JGB hit a record 4.17% and the 10Y reached 2.8% (29-yr high) after PM Takaichi ordered a multi-trillion-yen supplementary budget on May 18. The US 10Y touched 4.63%, highest since Feb 2025.

explainer.tagline: JGBs lead a global long-end rout — Japan 30Y at record 4.17%, US 10Y at 4.63%, on oil + Takaichi's fiscal pivot.
explainer.whatIsIt: Japan's 30-year JGB yield rose to 4.17% on May 18, the highest since the tenor began trading in 1999. The 10-year JGB hit roughly 2.80%, a 29-year high. The US 10-year Treasury followed, climbing to about 4.63% intraday — its highest level since February 2025.
explainer.howItWorks: The proximate driver was PM Sanae Takaichi's request to Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama to compile a supplementary budget 'on the scale of several trillion yen' to extend gasoline subsidies and offset summer utility-bill increases. The framing of new deficit-covering issuance, layered on top of Brent above $110/bbl from the Iran/Hormuz situation, repriced inflation risk and term premium across long-end curves. Tokyo equities (Nikkei, TOPIX) and US Treasury futures both sold off through the morning.
explainer.whyItMatters: The 30Y JGB is the world's archetypal anchor for long-duration risk — once that breaks higher, life-insurer hedging, FX-hedged Treasury demand from Japanese accounts and global duration positioning all have to be re-marked. The move also sets up an awkward backdrop for the G7 finance ministers meeting in Paris May 18–19, which has bond-market volatility on the agenda, and complicates Kevin Warsh's first week as Fed Chair with the US 10Y now within ~40bp of 5%.
explainer.forWho: rates desks, JPY-funding traders, global macro

image.alt: Japan PM Sanae Takaichi at a ruling-coalition meeting on May 18, 2026 where she ordered a supplementary budget

items.title: NextEra to buy Dominion in all-stock ~$67B mega-merger — creates world's largest regulated utility, locks in AI data-center power
items.summary: NextEra and Dominion confirm all-stock deal: 0.8138 NEE per D share + $360M cash pool, 74.5% / 25.5% ownership. Combined company has ~10M customers across FL/VA/NC/SC and 110 GW generation. Targets 9%+ adj EPS growth through 2032; close H2 2027.

explainer.tagline: Largest US power M&A on record — NextEra buys Dominion all-stock to corner Northern Virginia's AI-power demand.
explainer.whatIsIt: NextEra Energy (NYSE: NEE) and Dominion Energy (NYSE: D) announced a definitive agreement on May 18 to combine in an all-stock transaction, confirming last week's Bloomberg-leaked talks. The combined company keeps the Dominion brand and serves about 10 million customers across Florida, Virginia, North Carolina and South Carolina with 110 GW of generation. Closing is expected in 12–18 months.
explainer.howItWorks: Each Dominion share converts to 0.8138 NEE shares plus a pro-rata cut of a one-time $360M cash payout. Pro-forma ownership is 74.5% NEE / 25.5% Dominion. NextEra CEO John Ketchum chairs and runs the combined company; Dominion's Robert Blue becomes president and CEO of regulated utilities. Customers get $2.25B in bill credits over two years post-close. The deal needs both shareholder votes, FERC, NRC, state PUC sign-offs in VA / NC / SC, and HSR antitrust clearance.
explainer.whyItMatters: This is the biggest US power M&A on record and pins together Dominion's Virginia/Carolinas footprint — which sits beneath 'Data Center Alley' — with NextEra's renewables, gas fleet and rate-base growth machine. Management targets 9%+ adj EPS growth through 2032 and 6% dividend growth through 2028 on an entity that will be more than 80% regulated. Read-throughs flow to other utility names (AEP, SO, DUK, AEE) on bid-ask for the next round of consolidation, and to natural-gas off-take if Dominion's pipeline buildout accelerates.

image.alt: NextEra Energy logo

items.title: Bitcoin slides to ~$77.6K as ~$700M liquidations and ~$1B weekly ETF outflows snap six-week inflow streak
items.summary: Bitcoin fell ~3% over 24h to a session low near $77,598 as a long-skewed liquidation cascade flushed leverage across majors. Spot BTC ETFs lost ~$1B for the week, ending a six-week inflow run that had pulled in ~$3.4B.

explainer.tagline: First serious leverage flush in months — sparked by surging real yields, not a single crypto-native catalyst.
explainer.whatIsIt: Bitcoin extended this week's slide overnight Friday-into-Saturday May 16, dipping to ~$77,598 (-3.2% on 24h) and dragging the majors with it. The move tracked the global bond rout that pushed the US 30Y to ~5.13% and triggered the worst US equity session since March.
explainer.howItWorks: A long-skewed liquidation cascade wiped out roughly $700M in derivatives positions over 24h, with ~$573M in long liquidations. Solana led the major-alt losers at -5.1%, ETH -3.7%, XRP -3.8%, BNB -4.5%. US spot Bitcoin ETFs shed about $1B for the week ending May 15, snapping a six-week inflow streak that had accumulated ~$3.4B. Total crypto market cap fell roughly $80B over two trading days.
explainer.whyItMatters: This is the cleanest rates-driven crypto drawdown of the cycle: no exchange failure, no protocol blow-up — just real yields above 5% pulling capital out of zero-yield risk assets. The ETF flow reversal is the bigger signal, since the six-week streak was the backbone of the rally off $70K. Watch the $74–75K and $70K support shelves; a break would put the spring rally fully in question.

image.alt: Bitcoin logo

items.title: Trump warns Iran 'clock is ticking' on Truth Social, convenes Situation Room Tuesday; WTI +1.75% in Sunday electronic trade
items.summary: Trump posted on Sunday that Iran's 'clock is ticking' and 'there won't be anything left of them' if it doesn't 'get moving, FAST'. Top national-security advisers will meet in the Situation Room Tuesday to weigh harder strikes; oil futures gapped higher on the open.

explainer.tagline: Sunday escalation puts the 'clock is ticking' label on Tehran and oil futures right ahead of Tuesday's military-options meeting.
explainer.whatIsIt: President Trump used Truth Social Sunday May 17 to warn Iran that 'the clock is ticking' and the US will 'hit much harder' if Tehran doesn't accept his terms, declaring 'there won't be anything left of them' if Iranian leaders don't 'get moving, FAST'. Axios reports a Situation Room meeting on Tuesday with Vice President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, envoy Steve Witkoff, and CIA Director John Ratcliffe to weigh new strike options.
explainer.howItWorks: The post follows a week in which crude posted its biggest weekly gain since the war began — Brent +~8%, WTI +~11% — as the Strait of Hormuz stays effectively closed and CENTCOM disclosed 81 vessels diverted plus 4 disabled during the Iranian-port blockade. On Sunday electronic crude trade, Brent jumped to $110.70 (+1.32%) and WTI to $107.26 (+1.75%). Pakistan and Qatar are mediating through Tehran.
explainer.whyItMatters: Oil moved hard Friday on inflation reports and the bond rout; weekend rhetoric and a Tuesday war-options meeting raise the odds Monday opens with another oil + Treasury-yield gap and renewed pressure on long-duration risk assets. A confirmed expansion of US strikes would reset the entire Q2 macro narrative — energy-driven CPI, FOMC reaction function, and dollar-yen — independent of corporate-earnings flow. Watch front-month crude, BZ vs CL spread, and 10Y breakevens at the open.

image.alt: President Trump speaking to reporters about Iran

items.title: U.S. April 2026 PPI — headline +6.0% Y/Y (highest since Dec 2022), M/M +1.4% vs +0.5% est; core +1.0% M/M nearly 3x est on energy + services spike
items.summary: Final-demand PPI rose 1.4% in April — largest monthly gain since March 2022 — pushing the YoY pace to 6.0%, far above the 0.5% M/M and 4.9% Y/Y consensus. Core PPI ran 1.0% M/M vs 0.4% est.

explainer.tagline: Wholesale prices surge to the hottest annual reading since 2022 as gasoline and services pipe through, one day after a hot CPI.
explainer.whatIsIt: The Bureau of Labor Statistics released the April 2026 Producer Price Index on Wednesday May 13. Final-demand PPI rose 1.4% on the month and 6.0% over the prior twelve months — the biggest monthly print since March 2022 and the highest annual rate since December 2022.
explainer.howItWorks: Headline PPI ran almost triple the 0.5% M/M consensus, and the 6.0% Y/Y handily beat the 4.9% poll. Core PPI (ex food + energy) rose 1.0% M/M versus 0.4% est. Roughly three-quarters of the goods gain traced to a 7.8% jump in final-demand energy, with a 15.6% surge in gasoline doing the heavy lifting as the Iran war keeps Brent above $100. But the services side was just as hot — final-demand services rose 1.2%, the biggest monthly increase since March 2022.
explainer.whyItMatters: Back-to-back hot CPI and PPI prints land hours after the Senate confirms Kevin Warsh as Fed chair and a day before Powell's term expires. The 10-year Treasury yield touched 4.49%, the highest since July, and Kalshi rate-hike odds for the next year more than doubled to ~35%. Equities split — tech grinds to fresh highs on AI flows while rates-sensitive sectors lag.
explainer.forWho: macro and rates desks tracking the Fed-transition trade

image.alt: Shoppers at a U.S. grocery store as April PPI hits 6.0% Y/Y

items.title: Cisco Q3 FY26 — revenue $15.8B (+12% Y/Y), non-GAAP EPS $1.06 beats $1.00 est; AI hyperscaler orders raised to $9B for FY26; ~4,000 layoffs and $1B restructuring; CSCO +17% AH
items.summary: Record-quarter revenue $15.8B (+12% Y/Y) and adj EPS $1.06 beat. FY26 AI-hyperscaler orders raised to $9B from $5B (4.5x FY25). FY26 EPS guide lifted to $4.27–$4.29; ~4,000 layoffs + $1B restructuring announced.

explainer.tagline: Networking giant smashes the quarter and quadruples its AI-hyperscaler order outlook, paying for the pivot with a $1B restructuring and ~4,000 job cuts.
explainer.whatIsIt: Cisco reported fiscal Q3 2026 results after the May 13 close. Revenue was a record $15.8B, up 12% Y/Y, with GAAP EPS of $0.85 and non-GAAP EPS of $1.06 versus $1.00 consensus. The company raised FY26 revenue to $62.8B–$63.0B and FY26 non-GAAP EPS to $4.27–$4.29 — its third consecutive quarterly guidance raise.
explainer.howItWorks: Total product orders climbed 35% and networking orders jumped 50%. Cisco said it has already taken $5.3B in AI-infrastructure orders from hyperscalers this fiscal year and now expects $9B in such orders for FY26 — 4.5x FY25 — with about $4B recognized as revenue. Alongside the print, the company announced a restructuring plan of up to $1B (about $450M in Q4) and disclosed it will cut fewer than 4,000 jobs, less than 5% of its workforce.
explainer.whyItMatters: Shares surged ~17% after-hours, a rare double-digit AH move for a mega-cap. The print confirms hyperscaler AI capex is still expanding — supportive for ANET, JNPR, NVDA and the broader networking complex — and pushes back on the 'AI orders are stalling' narrative. Watch CSCO's reaction at Thursday's open against the cooling US-equity tape after the hot CPI/PPI prints.
explainer.forWho: AI-infrastructure investors, dividend funds, large-cap tech desks

items.title: Alibaba Q4 FY26 — revenue RMB243.4B (+3% Y/Y) misses, but Cloud Intelligence +38% on AI; non-GAAP net income RMB86M ($12M) collapses 100% Y/Y on AI capex + quick-commerce losses; BABA -3%
items.summary: Revenue RMB243.4B ($35.3B) up 3% Y/Y. Cloud Intelligence revenue surged 38% on AI products with the 11th straight triple-digit AI-revenue quarter. But adj net income collapsed from RMB29.8B to RMB86M as AI infrastructure spend and Quick Commerce losses bit.

explainer.tagline: Alibaba's March-quarter AI cloud and quick-commerce growth is real — but capex spend and food-delivery losses crush adjusted profit to near zero.
explainer.whatIsIt: Alibaba reported fiscal Q4 (March quarter) 2026 results on May 13. Revenue of RMB243.4B ($35.3B) grew 3% Y/Y, accelerating to 11% on a like-for-like basis excluding disposed businesses. The headline result: non-GAAP net income fell ~100% from RMB29.85B to RMB86M ($12M), and the company posted its first operating loss since 2021.
explainer.howItWorks: Cloud Intelligence revenue rose 38% Y/Y to RMB41.6B, with AI-related product revenue at RMB8.97B and an eleventh consecutive triple-digit Y/Y growth quarter. China commerce customer management revenue grew 1% (+8% LFL). Quick commerce revenue surged 57%, but the 'All others' segment swung to a RMB21.2B EBITA loss on AI capex and Qwen app acquisition spend. Free cash flow swung to an outflow of RMB46.6B for the year. The board declared an annual dividend of $0.13125/share (~$2.5B).
explainer.whyItMatters: BABA traded down ~3% pre-market as adjusted earnings missed by a wide margin. The print frames the China big-tech AI debate the same way US hyperscaler capex debates went last year: cloud is growing, but profits are now hostage to the capex cycle and quick-commerce price war. Read-across to PDD and JD on quick commerce, and to AMD/NVDA on China AI demand.
explainer.forWho: China tech desks, AI infrastructure analysts, EM growth funds

items.title: Senate confirms Kevin Warsh as next Fed Chair 54-45 — narrowest margin in Fed history; takes over from Powell on May 15 with Powell staying as governor through Jan 2028
items.summary: Senate voted 54-45 along party lines (Fetterman only crossover) to confirm Kevin Warsh as Fed chair on May 13, the narrowest confirmation margin in Fed history. Replaces Powell on May 15; Powell stays on as governor until DOJ Fed-renovation probe closes.

explainer.tagline: Warsh inherits the Fed chair as the May CPI and PPI come in hot — and Powell sticks around as governor through 2028, an unusual setup.
explainer.whatIsIt: The U.S. Senate voted 54-45 on Wednesday May 13 to confirm former Fed governor Kevin Warsh as the next chair of the Federal Reserve Board, replacing Jerome Powell whose chair term expires Friday May 15. Only Pennsylvania Democrat John Fetterman crossed party lines to vote yes; the margin is the narrowest for any Fed chair confirmation in history.
explainer.howItWorks: Warsh served as a Fed governor from 2006 to 2011, was on the FOMC during the 2008 financial crisis, and was a Morgan Stanley M&A banker before that. Powell announced April 29 he would remain on the Board as a governor — a seat that runs through January 2028 — at least until the DOJ closes its investigation into the Fed's $2.5B headquarters renovation project. That makes Warsh the first chair in nearly 80 years to inherit the role with his predecessor still on the board.
explainer.whyItMatters: Warsh takes over with back-to-back hot inflation prints (April CPI +3.8% Y/Y, April PPI +6.0% Y/Y) and Fed-funds futures pricing essentially zero 2026 cuts. He's perceived as more hawkish than Powell, but markets are now watching how he balances Trump's public push for cuts against the inflation data. The 10-year yield touched a 2026 high of 4.49% on the day.
explainer.forWho: rates traders, dollar/EM macro desks, Fed-watchers

image.alt: Kevin Warsh during Federal Reserve chair confirmation week

items.title: FCC approves EchoStar's $40B spectrum sale on May 13 — AT&T pays $23B for 50MHz, SpaceX pays $17B for 65MHz; SATS +5.7% to $134.74
items.summary: FCC cleared EchoStar's twin spectrum sales May 13: AT&T gets ~50MHz mid-band + low-band for $23B to expand 5G; SpaceX gets ~65MHz for $17B for direct-to-cell Starlink. EchoStar must post a $2.4B escrow for license disputes.

explainer.tagline: FCC unlocks the largest single spectrum sale in U.S. history — $40B split between AT&T's 5G build and SpaceX's direct-to-cell Starlink push.
explainer.whatIsIt: The U.S. Federal Communications Commission on Wednesday May 13 approved EchoStar's twin spectrum sales, totaling about $40B. AT&T pays $23B for roughly 50MHz of mid-band and low-band spectrum to extend its 5G coverage. SpaceX pays $17B for about 65MHz of EchoStar's higher-band spectrum to power its next-generation direct-to-cell Starlink service.
explainer.howItWorks: The FCC permits SpaceX to use the acquired spectrum for 'terrestrial, space-based and hybrid network architectures' — a meaningful broadening of how satellite operators can use cellular spectrum. AT&T is subject to accelerated build-out timelines, faster than auction defaults. AT&T and EchoStar also signed a hybrid MVNO arrangement so Boost Mobile customers stay served. EchoStar must establish a $2.4B escrow account to cover any contested obligations tied to the licenses, which EchoStar called an 'unprecedented involuntary escrow condition.'
explainer.whyItMatters: EchoStar shares rose ~5.7% to $134.74 from Tuesday's $129.38 close. The deal recapitalizes EchoStar after years of debt stress and pours new spectrum into both AT&T's terrestrial 5G and SpaceX's direct-to-cell. Read-through to TMUS (incumbent direct-to-cell partner with Starlink), AAPL/GOOGL (carrier negotiations), and the satellite-comms complex (ASTS, IRDM).
explainer.forWho: telecom analysts, satellite-comms desks, AT&T/SpaceX strategy watchers

items.title: Birkenstock FQ2 2026 — revenue €618M (+14% CC) tops on Americas + APAC strength but misses Street; adj EPS €0.50 (-9%) misses €0.59 est; Iran war cuts EMEA by €6M and tariffs trim margin 90bp; BIRK -5.5%
items.summary: Revenue €618M (+8% reported / +14% constant-currency). Americas +14%, EMEA +11%, APAC +30%. Adj EPS €0.50 missed €0.59 est. Mideast war cut EMEA by €6M and US tariffs trimmed gross margin 90bp; FY26 13–15% revenue growth target reaffirmed.

explainer.tagline: Birkenstock keeps its 13–15% FY revenue target intact even with the Iran war hitting Europe and U.S. tariffs trimming margins.
explainer.whatIsIt: Birkenstock reported fiscal Q2 2026 (March quarter) results on May 13. Revenue was €618M, up 8% reported and 14% in constant currency. Americas grew 14% CC, EMEA 11% CC, and APAC a standout 30% CC. Adjusted EBITDA was €198M with a 32.1% margin, and adjusted EPS of €0.50 missed the €0.59 Street estimate by ~15%.
explainer.howItWorks: Management quantified two specific drags: 'conflicts in the Middle East' reduced EMEA revenue by roughly €6M, and U.S. tariffs cut gross margin by about 90bp. Unfavorable FX translation cost another 230–240bp of margin. The company reaffirmed its FY26 outlook: 13–15% revenue growth in constant currency, at least €700M of adjusted EBITDA, an adjusted EBITDA margin of 30.0–30.5%, and adjusted EPS of €1.90–€2.05. Reported revenue is guided to €2.3B–€2.35B.
explainer.whyItMatters: BIRK fell ~5.5% intraday — one of the bigger consumer-discretionary moves on May 13. The print is a real-world stress test of the post-Iran-war global consumer trade: tariffs are biting, Mideast EMEA is softening, but Americas and APAC growth still hit double-digits. Read-through to other premium-priced lifestyle brands (LULU, ONON, DECK) with similar geo mix.

image.alt: Birkenstock fiscal Q2 2026 results cover image

items.title: Doximity FQ4 2026 — revenue $145M (+5% Y/Y) tops est but adj EPS $0.26 misses $0.28; FY27 revenue guide $664M–$676M well below $697M consensus; DOCS -11.6%
items.summary: Q4 revenue $145.4M (+5% Y/Y) beat $144M est. Adj EBITDA margin 45%. But adj EPS $0.26 missed $0.28 est, FY27 revenue guide $664–$676M sits well below $697M consensus and FY27 EBITDA margin guide of 49% prints flat. DOCS -11.6%.

explainer.tagline: Doximity beats Q4 revenue but issues an FY27 guide that pegs growth at ~4% — the slowdown spooks the AI-physician growth bull thesis.
explainer.whatIsIt: Doximity reported fiscal Q4 2026 (March quarter) and full-year results on May 13. Q4 revenue was $145.4M, up 5% Y/Y and slightly above the $144M consensus. Free cash flow hit $107M — the company's first 9-digit FCF quarter. But adjusted EPS of $0.26 missed the $0.28 consensus, and forward guidance came in soft.
explainer.howItWorks: Q1 FY27 revenue is guided to $151M–$152M versus $153.7M est. Full-year FY27 revenue is guided to $664M–$676M (~4% growth at midpoint) versus $697M consensus, with adjusted EBITDA of $323M–$335M and a 49% margin (flat vs FY26's 55%). The bright spots: 800k+ active prescribers using workflow tools in Q4, with nearly half engaging Doximity's clinical AI and prompts-per-user nearly doubling January-to-April. New partnerships with Aledade and Photon roll the Clinical AI Suite into independent practices.
explainer.whyItMatters: Shares dropped ~11.6% on the FY27 guide, extending DOCS's drawdown to ~40% YTD. The print frames the digital-health-platform debate: the AI engagement story is real, but it's not yet translating into a revenue re-acceleration. Read-through to other clinician-workflow names and physician-marketing budgets at pharma.

image.alt: Doximity FQ4 and FY 2026 results cover

items.title: Nebius Q1 2026 — revenue $399M (+684% Y/Y) blows past $379M est, net income $621.2M; secures 1.2GW Pennsylvania AI factory, NBIS +14% intraday
items.summary: AI cloud's first Q1 as a public reporting standalone: revenue $399M (~7× Y/Y), adj EBITDA $129.5M flips from loss, and a 1.2 GW Pennsylvania site builds the next leg of the GPU buildout. NBIS +14%.

explainer.tagline: Neocloud Nebius posts a 684% revenue surge, swings adj EBITDA positive, and locks 1.2 GW for a Pennsylvania AI factory.
explainer.whatIsIt: Nebius Group, the AI cloud company spun out of Yandex N.V. and relisted on Nasdaq as NBIS, reported its first-quarter 2026 results before the open on May 13. The print sent shares up ~12% in premarket and ~14% intraday to a record, with the stock now well clear of its early-2026 lows on top of a strong run since its $643M Eigen AI acquisition on May 1.
explainer.howItWorks: Q1 revenue came in at $399.0M, up 684% from $50.9M a year ago and ~$20M above the $379M consensus. Net income from continuing operations was $621.2M ($2.40 basic / $2.11 diluted), boosted by a non-cash mark on equity holdings. Adjusted EBITDA flipped to +$129.5M from -$53.7M Y/Y, and operating cash flow was $2.26B. Separately, Nebius said it has secured up to 1.2 gigawatts of power and land for a new owned AI factory in Pennsylvania.
explainer.whyItMatters: Nebius is one of three pure-play AI-cloud comps alongside CoreWeave and Lambda; the print confirms that hyperscale demand for rented Nvidia capacity is still outrunning supply rather than rolling over. The 1.2 GW Pennsylvania commitment puts NBIS in the same on-shore power-and-land race as CRWV and the cloud arms of MSFT, GOOG, AMZN — and the Eigen AI tuck-in points to a software/services layer on top of the bare-metal GPU rental business.

image.alt: Nebius Group Q1 2026 results share image

items.title: Under Armour FQ4 FY26 — revenue $1.17B (-1% rep / -4% CC), adj loss $0.03/sh misses; FY27 EPS guide $0.08–$0.12 vs $0.23 est; UAA -17%
items.summary: Under Armour reported a wider-than-expected Q4 loss as gross margin contracted 470bp to 42% and initial FY27 EPS guide of $0.08–$0.12 came in well below consensus, sending UAA ~17% lower.

explainer.tagline: Q4 loss wider than feared and FY27 guide undershoots Street by half — UAA drops to $5.03.
explainer.whatIsIt: Under Armour closed fiscal 2026 with a Q4 net loss of $43M on revenue of $1.17B, capping a year in which sales fell 4% to $5.0B. CEO Kevin Plank framed the initial FY27 outlook as a deliberate reset toward 'controlled and predictable' execution.
explainer.howItWorks: Q4 revenue dropped 0.8% reported (-4.2% constant currency) with North America down 8% offset partly by EMEA +9% and LatAm +9%. Gross margin compressed 470bp to 42.0% on tariff and cost pressure, producing a GAAP diluted loss of $0.10 and adjusted loss of $0.03 vs the $0.02 consensus loss. For FY27 the company guided to a slight revenue decline, gross margin +220–270bp, adjusted operating income of $140–$160M, and adjusted EPS of $0.08–$0.12 — roughly half the $0.23 Street estimate.
explainer.whyItMatters: The reset cements Under Armour as a North America turnaround story rather than a growth name and contrasts with On Holding's record CHF 832M quarter the same week. Apparel and footwear traders are recalibrating expectations for a fiscal-year recovery that now arrives later and starts from a smaller base, while tariff-driven gross-margin pressure remains a sector-wide read-through.

image.alt: Under Armour corporate logo

items.title: Zebra Technologies Q1 2026 — revenue $1.495B (+14.3%), non-GAAP EPS $4.75 beats $4.26 est; raises FY26 EPS guide to $18.30–$18.70; ZBRA ~+19%
items.summary: Zebra topped Q1 estimates on 14% revenue growth, Connected Frontline +20.6%, adjusted EBITDA margin +90bp to 23.2%, and raised the FY26 EPS guide above Street; ZBRA spiked 19% intraday.

explainer.tagline: Industrial-scanning leader posts double-digit revenue growth, raises full-year guide above Street — ZBRA up as much as 19%.
explainer.whatIsIt: Zebra Technologies, the dominant supplier of barcode scanners, mobile computers, RFID and machine-vision gear for warehouses and retail, reported Q1 2026 results that beat top and bottom-line estimates and raised the full-year outlook. The quarter included the first full period of the Elo Touch acquisition.
explainer.howItWorks: Net sales rose 14.3% Y/Y to $1.495B (4.3% organic), with Connected Frontline up 20.6% on Elo and mobile computing strength and Asset Visibility & Automation up 4.8%. Adjusted gross margin expanded 80bp to 50.4%, adj EBITDA margin +90bp to 23.2%, and non-GAAP diluted EPS hit $4.75 vs $4.26 est. FY26 guide moves to 10–14% sales growth and $18.30–$18.70 non-GAAP EPS (above the $17.50 consensus) with free cash flow >$900M; Zebra also flagged $300M of Q1 buybacks.
explainer.whyItMatters: Zebra is a real-economy AI read-through — its scanners and machine-vision sensors are what factories and retailers buy when they actually deploy automation. The double-digit organic + acquisition growth and raised guide push back on tariff/margin concerns that hit names like Under Armour the same morning and reinforce the manufacturing-end-market strength that's already been visible in other industrial-tech prints.

image.alt: Zebra Technologies Q1 2026 results announcement

items.title: U.S. April 2026 CPI — headline +3.8% Y/Y (highest since May 2023) beats 3.7% est; core +2.8% Y/Y vs 2.7% est on energy spike
items.summary: April CPI rose 0.6% M/M, putting the YoY pace at 3.8% — the hottest annual reading since May 2023 and one tenth above the 3.7% Dow Jones consensus. Core CPI rose 0.4% M/M and 2.8% Y/Y, both one tenth hot on Iran-driven energy.

explainer.tagline: April CPI runs hotter than expected on both headline and core, with the annual rate climbing to the highest reading in nearly three years.
explainer.whatIsIt: The Bureau of Labor Statistics released the April 2026 CPI on Tuesday May 12 at 8:30am ET. Headline CPI rose 0.6% on the month — the largest monthly gain since the 2022 inflation peak — and 3.8% over the prior twelve months. Core CPI, which strips out food and energy, rose 0.4% M/M and 2.8% Y/Y.
explainer.howItWorks: The monthly headline rate matched the Dow Jones consensus, but the YoY pace came in one tenth above the 3.7% poll and is the highest annual reading since May 2023. Core was hotter than the 0.3% M/M and 2.7% Y/Y estimates. Energy drove the print: the energy index rose 3.8% on the month, accounting for more than 40% of the headline increase, on the back of the Iran/Israel oil shock. With this release, several CPI series were rebased to December 2024 = 100.
explainer.whyItMatters: This is the second straight hot CPI print and lands as Kevin Warsh advances through Senate votes to replace Jerome Powell, whose term expires May 15. Fed-funds futures priced essentially zero 2026 rate cuts ahead of the release; a sticky 2.8% core combined with energy passthrough keeps the cuts-priced-out trade intact. The 10-year, 30-year and DXY all reactive; equity futures faded into the release as the stagflation tape stays in play.
explainer.forWho: macro and rates desks, asset allocators tracking the Fed-transition trade

image.alt: Shoppers at a US grocery store as April CPI rises to 3.8% Y/Y

items.title: JD.com Q1 2026 — net revenue RMB315.7B (+4.9% Y/Y) tops, non-GAAP EPS $0.74 beats $0.54 est, but food-delivery losses cut net income in half to RMB5.1B
items.summary: Revenue grew 4.9% to RMB315.7B (~$45.8B); JD Retail op margin expanded 70bp to 5.6% on record profitability. But New Businesses (food delivery) lost RMB10.4B, sending GAAP net income to RMB5.1B vs RMB10.9B a year ago.

explainer.tagline: Core e-commerce profitability hits a record while a costly food-delivery offensive cuts group net income in half.
explainer.whatIsIt: JD.com reported Q1 2026 results before US open on May 12. Total net revenue grew 4.9% Y/Y to RMB315.7B (~$45.8B), with service revenues accelerating 20.6%. JD Retail set a record for Q1 profitability with op margin expanding to 5.6% from 4.9%, but the company's push into food delivery via the New Businesses segment produced a RMB10.4B operating loss, dragging consolidated GAAP net income down 53% to RMB5.1B.
explainer.howItWorks: Non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.74, beating the $0.54 consensus by $0.20. Non-GAAP net income halved to RMB7.4B from RMB12.8B as New Businesses outweighed Retail and Logistics. JD Logistics revenue was RMB60.6B with a 1.7% op margin. CEO Sandy Xu said annual active customers hit a record and that New Businesses showed 'meaningful bottom-line improvements' sequentially. JD opened in premarket trading up roughly 1% on the EPS beat after sliding into the print.
explainer.whyItMatters: JD's food-delivery push is the direct response to Meituan and Alibaba's instant-commerce build-outs, but the Q1 hit confirms the cash cost of that fight. Investors now have to underwrite a parallel China consumer business plus a multi-billion-RMB quarterly burn until New Businesses gets to scale. Read-throughs: Meituan (3690.HK) and Alibaba (BABA) on competitive intensity in instant commerce; PDD on whether the Q1 RMB-deflation backdrop is bottoming.

image.alt: JD.com warehouse with workers preparing parcels for delivery

items.title: Sea Limited Q1 2026 — revenue $7.1B (+46.6%) beats $6.4B est, net income $438M; Shopee GMV +30% to record $37.3B and Monee +57.8%
items.summary: Sea posted Q1 revenue $7.1B (+46.6%) handily ahead of $6.4B consensus, with Shopee GMV at a record $37.3B (+30%), Monee revenue +57.8% to $1.2B and Garena +40.6%. Net income +6.7% to $438M and the company repurchased 1.8M shares.

explainer.tagline: Shopee, Monee and Garena all post their strongest quarters on record as Sea's flywheel re-accelerates.
explainer.whatIsIt: Sea Limited reported Q1 2026 results before US open on May 12, with GAAP revenue of $7.1B up 46.6% Y/Y and well ahead of the $6.4B Zacks consensus. All three segments contributed: Shopee revenue $5.1B (+44.4%) on a record $37.3B GMV and 4.0 billion orders, Monee revenue $1.2B (+57.8%) on credit-business growth, and Garena $697M (+40.6%) as Free Fire and the gaming portfolio re-accelerated.
explainer.howItWorks: Group net income rose 6.7% to $438.2M and adjusted EBITDA was $1.0B (+9.3%). Diluted EPS came in at $0.67 vs $0.65 Y/Y. Shopee adjusted EBITDA was $223.2M with order volume up 29.3% Y/Y, GMV per active buyer expanding, and unit economics stable. Sea repurchased 1.8 million shares during the quarter.
explainer.whyItMatters: Sea's Q4 print on March 3 had sent the stock down ~15% as the market questioned whether Shopee's growth was rolling. Q1's record GMV, 30% growth and continued profit at the Shopee level reverse that narrative cleanly. Read-throughs: SE-Asia consumer is still expanding (compare Grab GRAB, GoTo); Monee's credit growth gives optionality on a fintech multiple; Garena's revival removes one of the bear cases on group margin mix.
explainer.forWho: EM and growth equity investors, Southeast Asia desks

items.title: On Holding Q1 2026 — net sales CHF 832M (+14.5% rep / +26.4% CC), adj EBITDA margin 21%; raises FY26 CC growth guide to ≥23%
items.summary: On posted its first ≥CHF 800M quarter with sales CHF 831.9M (+14.5% rep, +26.4% CC), adjusted EBITDA margin expanding 450bp to 21.0%, and net income +82% to CHF 103.3M. FY26 guidance raised to ≥23% CC sales growth.

explainer.tagline: On crosses CHF 800M in a quarter for the first time, with APAC up 61% and the FY26 growth guide bumped to 23%+.
explainer.whatIsIt: On Holding reported Q1 2026 results before US open on May 12. Net sales of CHF 831.9M grew 14.5% on a reported basis and 26.4% in constant currency — the first quarter above CHF 800M in the company's history. Adjusted EBITDA margin expanded 450 basis points to 21.0% and net income jumped 82% Y/Y to CHF 103.3M.
explainer.howItWorks: Asia-Pacific revenue rose 61.4% on a constant-currency basis, with apparel up 57.5% CC, both running well ahead of group. Gross margin held at 64.2%. The company raised FY26 guidance to at least 23% constant-currency net-sales growth (from prior 27% growth in CC sales translated to a more conservative reported view), gross-profit margin at least 64.5%, and adjusted EBITDA margin 19.5–20.0%. CHF strength versus the dollar provides a multi-hundred-basis-point headwind to reported numbers.
explainer.whyItMatters: Nike and Deckers (HOKA) both flagged slowing premium running demand earlier this year; On's data argues the slowdown is brand-specific, not category-wide. The print sets up a wider premium-vs-mass divide in performance footwear and validates On's expansion runway in apparel and APAC. ONON had closed Monday down 3.4% to $34.04; options had been pricing a ±9.66% move.

image.alt: On Holding Q1 2026 press release header image

items.title: Senate confirms Kevin Warsh to Fed Board of Governors 51–45 on May 12; final chair-confirmation vote teed up for May 13 ahead of Powell's May 15 expiry
items.summary: Warsh wins his Board seat 51–45 (Fetterman the only Democrat in favor) and the Senate immediately moves to limit debate on the separate chair vote, expected May 13. Powell's chair term ends Friday.

explainer.tagline: Warsh is one procedural step away from a Fed Board seat and is expected to be confirmed as Chair later this week.
explainer.whatIsIt: The U.S. Senate voted 49–44 on Monday evening to invoke cloture on Kevin Warsh's nomination to the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, advancing past a procedural hurdle. A confirmation roll-call was scheduled for Tuesday May 12, with a separate vote to confirm Warsh as Chair — succeeding Jerome Powell — expected later in the week. Powell's term as Chair expires May 15.
explainer.howItWorks: Warsh would first take a 14-year seat as Governor beginning February 1, then succeed Powell as Chair if confirmed. The Senate Banking Committee had previously approved his nomination on largely party lines on April 29. Warsh, a former Fed Governor (2006–2011) under Bernanke, has signaled a preference for a smaller Fed balance sheet and a higher bar for rate cuts, broadly aligning with the Trump administration's policy mix.
explainer.whyItMatters: The transition lands the same week as a hot April CPI print, with futures pricing essentially zero 2026 cuts. Warsh's confirmation removes the last source of Fed-chair uncertainty and pins the early-cycle reaction function on his prior hawkish tilt and balance-sheet preferences. Watch the 10Y (recently 4.41%), the 30Y (above 5% for the first time since July 2025), DXY and SPX as the chair vote completes.

image.alt: Kevin Warsh testifies before the Senate Banking Committee

items.title: Hims & Hers Q1 2026 — revenue $608M (+4% Y/Y) but GLP-1 pivot drives $92.1M net loss; gross margin -800bp to 65% and HIMS drops ~10%
items.summary: Q1 revenue $608M (+4%) misses on growth slowdown as compounded-semaglutide wind-down hits margins. Net loss $92.1M / -$0.40 EPS reverses $49.5M prior-year profit; FY26 sales guide raised to $2.8–3.0B but EBITDA guide $275–350M.

explainer.tagline: Strategic pivot away from compounded semaglutide collapses margins as Hims pivots to branded GLP-1s from Novo and Lilly.
explainer.whatIsIt: Hims & Hers reported Q1 2026 earnings after the close on May 11. Revenue of $608.1M grew just 4% Y/Y against tougher comps, and the company swung from a $49.5M profit a year ago to a $92.1M net loss as the company exits compounded GLP-1 and transitions to branded supply.
explainer.howItWorks: Gross margin compressed to 65% (from 73%) on the GLP-1 mix shift, and adj EBITDA fell to $44.3M from $91.1M. Q1 took $33.5M in restructuring charges plus $17.6M of fair-value losses on liabilities and $9.7M on equity securities. Subscribers grew 9% to ~2.6M, but ASPs fell. Management raised FY26 revenue guide to $2.8–3.0B and set adj EBITDA guide at $275–350M.
explainer.whyItMatters: HIMS gapped ~10% lower after-hours as the print confirmed the compounded-GLP-1 cash cow is gone faster than the branded partnerships can replace it. The Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly deals will eventually broaden reach, but near-term margin compression makes the 'platform' story expensive to underwrite. items.title: Fervo Energy upsizes IPO to ~$1.82B — 70M shares at $25–$26 (up from 55.6M at $21–$24); FRVO targets Nasdaq debut at up to ~$7.4B
items.summary: Bill Gates-backed geothermal developer Fervo Energy upsized its Nasdaq IPO Monday: 70M Class A shares at $25–$26 (vs prior 55.6M at $21–$24), targeting up to $1.82B in proceeds at a $7.4B implied valuation.

explainer.tagline: Fervo's geothermal IPO got upsized 26% with the price range raised — the largest climate-tech listing of 2026 is heading into Nasdaq pricing with 20×+ demand.
explainer.whatIsIt: Fervo Energy is the Houston-based enhanced-geothermal (EGS) developer building the 500MW+ Cape Station project in Utah for AI-data-center customers including Google. Monday, Fervo filed an upsized S-1/A on EDGAR raising its IPO to 70.0M Class A shares at $25–$26, up from 55.6M at $21–$24 a week ago. At the top of the new range the deal would raise $1.82B and value Fervo at ~$7.4B — versus the $4B private valuation in its December 2025 $462M Series E.
explainer.howItWorks: The new terms: 70M shares (a 14.4M share upsize) at $25–$26, plus a 30-day option for underwriters to sell another 10.5M shares. JPMorgan, BofA, RBC and Barclays are joint lead bookrunners; the company has applied to list on Nasdaq under 'FRVO'. Bloomberg, citing the SEC filing, said the upsize reflects 20×+ oversubscription. Prior backers in the Series E included B Capital, Breakthrough Energy Ventures (Bill Gates), Devon Energy, and Google.
explainer.whyItMatters: Fervo would be the largest US climate-tech IPO of 2026 and lands the same week as Cerebras (CBRS), which separately upsized its book to $150–$160 / up to $4.8B. Together they signal the AI-power thesis (chips + 24/7 firm clean energy for hyperscaler load) is rapidly opening the IPO window after a multi-year drought. Comp set: NUS, OKLO, VST, GEV. Risk: an upsized print at a 75%+ valuation jump in five months is the kind of book that has reversed hard post-debut in prior cycles (e.g. Cava).

items.title: Santa Clara County sues Meta over Facebook/Instagram scam ads — complaint alleges up to $7B/yr in 'high-risk' fraud-ad revenue
items.summary: Santa Clara County filed suit in California Superior Court Monday alleging Meta knowingly profited from scam ads on Facebook and Instagram, citing internal docs that pegged 'high-risk' fraud-ad revenue at up to $7B/yr.

metrics.Filed: Mon May 11, Santa Clara Co. Superior Ct.
metrics.Plaintiff: County of Santa Clara (on behalf of CA residents)

explainer.tagline: Santa Clara County sued Meta in California Superior Court Monday over Facebook and Instagram scam ads, citing leaked internal documents that put 'high-risk' fraud-ad revenue at up to $7B/yr.
explainer.whatIsIt: The complaint, filed Monday in Santa Clara County Superior Court on behalf of all California residents, accuses Meta of violating California's false-advertising and unfair-business-practices laws by tolerating fraudulent ads on Facebook and Instagram. County Counsel Tony LoPresti is pursuing the case with three outside firms (Bernstein Litowitz, Renne Public Law Group, Bishop Partnoy) on a contingency basis.
explainer.howItWorks: Citing internal documents previously reported by Reuters, the county alleges Meta earned up to $7B in annual revenue from 'high-risk' scam ads with clear signs of fraud, and that — rather than blocking the worst advertisers — Meta charged them higher rates. The suit also alleges Meta let middlemen resell protected ad accounts, targeted scam ads at users who had clicked similar ads before, and that its generative AI systems helped fraudsters draft ads. Relief sought: restitution, civil damages, and an injunction against the practices.
explainer.whyItMatters: This is a state-court consumer-protection case, not a federal action, but it follows a New Mexico DOJ verdict and a CFA federal suit and tees up a possible multi-state pattern. Meta's response: it removed 159M scam ads last year and took down 10.9M accounts. META barely moved on the news, but the case spotlights ad-platform liability for AI-assisted fraud — a risk vector the Street has not yet priced into '26 ad revenue.
explainer.forWho: Mega-cap tech, ad-tech, and platform-regulation analysts

image.alt: Meta Platforms logo at a corporate building, accompanying Reuters coverage of the Santa Clara County lawsuit over Facebook and Instagram scam ads.

items.title: Roundhill Memory ETF (DRAM) becomes fastest US ETF ever to $5B AUM — record $1.1B single-day inflow; +13.4% Friday close as AI memory rally accelerates
items.summary: DRAM, Roundhill's pure-play memory-chip ETF, crossed $5B in AUM 25 trading days after its April 2 launch — the fastest of any US ETF on record, beating spot bitcoin ETFs. Up ~70% since launch, AUM ~$6.25B by May 10.

explainer.tagline: Roundhill's memory-chip ETF DRAM became the fastest US ETF ever to $5B AUM — a clean readout on how hard the AI-memory trade is being chased.
explainer.whatIsIt: DRAM is an actively managed ETF run by Roundhill Investments that gives pure-play exposure to global memory-chip names — DRAM, HBM, NAND, and storage. It launched April 2, 2026 with the AI-memory bottleneck thesis (Samsung and SK Hynix together represent nearly half the fund). On Thursday May 7 it pulled in a record $1.1B in a single day, crossing $5B in AUM after just 25 trading sessions.
explainer.howItWorks: Top holdings are Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix (largest weights), Micron, Kioxia, and SanDisk. AUM growth: $1B in 10 trading days, $2.5B by early May, $5B on May 7, ~$6.25B by May 10. Expense ratio 0.65%, active management. The fund closed Friday May 8 up +13.43% at $52.80 as Micron and SanDisk each gained more than 15% on the AI-memory rally and a stronger-than-expected April jobs report.
explainer.whyItMatters: The pace beats every prior ETF launch including the spot bitcoin ETFs of January 2024 — VettaFi's head of research called it 'one of the most successful ETF launches in history.' Flow concentration tells the same story as Samsung crossing $1T market cap and Micron at $700B+: the AI-bottleneck is widely diagnosed as memory, and capital is piling into the cleanest vehicle. Risk: a fund this concentrated in two Korean names and Micron magnifies any pullback in the memory cycle.

items.summary: Barrick Mining posted adj EPS $0.98 (+180% Y/Y) on $5.22B revenue (+67% Y/Y), with 719k oz of gold topping the 640–680k oz guide; Board authorized a $3.0B share repurchase and $0.175 quarterly dividend.

The $3B buyback is roughly 7% of the company's market cap on top of the dividend, and management reaffirmed FY26 guidance (2.90–3.25 Moz gold, 190–220kt copper) while flagging the planned North American Barrick IPO is still on track for year-end. Watch ^GSPC chip names (NVDA, MU, AVGO) for follow-through and a potentially crowded long-Korea trade if the 8,000 print arrives this week.

items.title: Nintendo plunges 9.9% in Tokyo to ¥6,908 — Switch 2 gets $50/¥10,000 price hike, FY27 profit guided -27% on memory-chip squeeze
items.summary: Nintendo announces Switch 2 price hikes across Japan, US, Canada and Europe and guides FY27 net profit -27% to ¥310B as AI-driven memory costs and Iran-war shipping bite; Tokyo shares -9.9% to ¥6,908.

explainer.whatIsIt: Nintendo on Friday May 8 announced sweeping price increases for the Switch 2 console across Japan, the U.S., Canada and Europe, alongside FY27 (Apr 2026–Mar 2027) guidance that called for net sales of ¥2.05T (-11.4% Y/Y) and net profit of ¥310B (-27% Y/Y), both well below LSEG consensus of ¥2.46T sales and ¥418.5B profit. items.title: China April 2026 inflation — PPI +2.8% Y/Y (45-month high) and CPI +1.2% Y/Y both blow past estimates as Iran-war oil shock feeds through
items.org: National Bureau of Statistics of China
items.summary: China's April PPI rose 2.8% Y/Y, the fastest pace in 45 months and well above a 1.5–1.8% Reuters/Bloomberg poll; CPI rose 1.2% Y/Y vs 0.9% est. The print confirms Iran-war oil costs are feeding through Chinese factories.

explainer.tagline: China's factory-gate prices jumped 2.8% Y/Y in April — a 45-month high — and consumer prices accelerated, confirming the Iran-war oil shock is bleeding into Asia's largest economy.
explainer.whatIsIt: The National Bureau of Statistics released April 2026 price data on Monday May 11. PPI rose 2.8% Y/Y (vs a 1.5–1.8% Reuters/Bloomberg poll) — the fastest pace since July 2022 and the second straight month of acceleration. CPI rose 1.2% Y/Y, beating a 0.9% estimate; on a monthly basis CPI ticked up 0.3% versus an expected 0.1% drop and March's 0.7% decline.
explainer.howItWorks: NBS statistician Dong Lijuan attributed the PPI jump to rising domestic petroleum-related prices tracking international moves, higher industrial demand, and improving competitive order. Production-material costs accelerated to +3.8% Y/Y from +1.0% in March, led by mining (+10.8% from +2.0%), raw materials (+7.1% from +1.1%) and processing (+1.5% from +0.9%). On a M/M basis, PPI rose 1.7% — the fastest since October 2021.
explainer.whyItMatters: After three years of producer-price deflation that pressured the PBOC to ease, China is now importing inflation through energy. The print narrows Beijing's room to cut rates or RRR ahead of the May 13–15 Trump-Xi summit and feeds the global stagflation narrative oil markets have been pricing since Hormuz disruptions began. Watch CNY/USD (currently ~7.07), 10Y CGB yields, and energy-exposed names like PetroChina (0857.HK) and Sinopec (0386.HK).

items.title: Trump rejects Iran's peace response as 'totally unacceptable' — Brent jumps +3.5% to ~$104.80, equity futures drop
items.summary: Trump rejects Iran's latest peace proposal — delivered via Pakistan on Sunday — as 'TOTALLY UNACCEPTABLE'. Brent oil futures jump as much as 3.5% to $104.80; Dow futures fall ~200 points.

explainer.tagline: Trump rejects Iran's Sunday peace response; oil jumps and US equity futures slide.
explainer.whatIsIt: Iran on Sunday May 10 sent its formal response to the U.S. peace proposal via Pakistani mediators, asking for the naval blockade to be lifted and rejecting U.S. demands to dismantle nuclear facilities and suspend uranium enrichment for 20 years. President Trump read the response and posted on Truth Social: 'I have just read the response from Iran's so-called Representatives. I don't like it — TOTALLY UNACCEPTABLE!' The 10-week U.S.-Iran war and Strait of Hormuz blockade therefore continue.
explainer.howItWorks: Iran's counter-proposal — relayed by Pakistan's IRNA — called for an immediate end to hostilities on all fronts including Lebanon, a halt to the U.S. naval blockade, sanctions relief and a 30-day window to negotiate nuclear issues. The U.S. proposal had demanded Iran cease enrichment for 12+ years and surrender 440kg of 60% enriched uranium in exchange for gradual sanctions relief. Iran's parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf said any ceasefire requires the U.S. lift its blockade first.
explainer.whyItMatters: Brent crude jumped as much as 3.5% to $104.80 a barrel and WTI rose to near $99, reversing last week's relief rally that took Brent below $100 on hopes of a deal. Dow futures fell ~200 points (-0.40%), S&P 500 futures -0.33% and Nasdaq -0.28% as Sunday electronic trading opened. Energy Secretary Chris Wright signaled the U.S. could resume military operations to reopen the Strait within 'the next few days' if diplomatic progress stalls, keeping the largest oil supply disruption in IEA history in play.
explainer.forWho: macro traders, oil desks, geopolitical risk

items.title: Saudi Aramco Q1 2026 — net income $32.5B, adj $33.6B (+26% Y/Y) beats; East-West pipeline running at 7 mb/d max as Hormuz stays blocked
items.summary: Aramco Q1 2026 — net income $32.5B beats $30.95B LSEG est, adj NI $33.6B (+26% Y/Y), revenue $115.5B (+7%); East-West pipeline at maximum 7 mb/d capacity as Strait of Hormuz blockade reroutes Saudi crude.

explainer.tagline: Aramco posts $33.6B adjusted profit (+26%) as Hormuz blockade reroutes Saudi crude through the East-West pipeline at full 7 mb/d capacity.
explainer.whatIsIt: Saudi Aramco — the world's largest oil exporter — reported Q1 2026 results on Sunday May 10. Net income hit $32.5B (+25% Y/Y) and adjusted net income reached $33.6B (+26% Y/Y), beating the LSEG consensus of $31.16B. Total revenue rose ~6.9% to $115.49B. CEO Amin Nasser said the East-West Pipeline — which crosses the Arabian Peninsula bypassing the Strait of Hormuz — 'reached its maximum capacity of 7.0 million barrels of oil per day.'
explainer.howItWorks: Higher realised prices ($76.90/bbl vs $76.30 Y/Y, $64.10 Q/Q) and higher crude/refined/chemical volumes drove the beat. Aramco declared a Q1 base dividend of SR82.08B ($21.89B), up 3.5% Y/Y, equal to SR0.3393 per share. Capex was $12.1B (down from $12.5B Y/Y). Free cash flow eased to $18.6B from $19.2B Y/Y. Gearing came in at 4.8%. Aramco also flagged it reduced output by ~2 mb/d in response to the Iran-related geopolitical disruption.
explainer.whyItMatters: Aramco is a proxy for global oil-supply economics in the Iran war: with the Strait of Hormuz blocked since late February, the East-West pipeline running at its theoretical max is the marginal hard cap on Saudi seaborne exports rerouted to the Red Sea. The $21.9B dividend underpins Saudi state finances even as crude prices whipsaw around $100-115 on ceasefire headlines. The beat-and-no-raise pattern keeps Aramco a high-yield defensive in an energy supercycle.

items.title: Forward Air Q1 2026 — discloses largest customer (~$250M / ~10% of revenue) shifting business to other providers; non-core asset sale plan; FWRD plunges ~45% to ~$9.50
items.summary: Forward Air's largest customer (~$250M / ~10% of 2025 revenue) is shifting business to other providers starting early 2027; strategic review yielded no full-company sale, board pursues asset sales. FWRD -45% AH.

explainer.tagline: Forward Air's biggest customer is leaving and the strategic-review sale process collapsed — stock loses nearly half its value.
explainer.whatIsIt: Forward Air reported Q1 2026 results alongside two material disclosures that crushed the stock: one of its largest customers, representing roughly $250M (about 10%) of 2025 revenue, plans to transition the majority of its business to other providers beginning in early 2027. Separately, the company's strategic-alternatives review produced no actionable proposals for a sale of the whole company, so the board has pivoted to selling non-core assets — the Intermodal segment and two smaller Omni businesses — to pay down debt.
explainer.howItWorks: Q1 revenue came in at $582.0M, down 5.1% Y/Y, with a net loss of $40.2M ($1.09 per share) versus a Zacks consensus loss of $0.35. Operating income improved to $20.4M from $4.8M a year ago and free cash flow rose to $40.2M. Liquidity ended the quarter at $402M ($141M cash + $261M revolver). Expedited Freight revenue grew 9.4% with a stable 10.4% EBITDA margin, while Intermodal revenue fell 15%. Shares closed the regular session at $17.33 and traded down ~45% to ~$9.50 in after-hours.
explainer.whyItMatters: The customer disclosure removes ~10% of forward revenue at a company already running a net loss and stretched balance sheet, and the failed sale process strips out the strategic-bid floor that retail had been pricing in. The asset-sale path (Intermodal + smaller Omni businesses) is now the deleveraging plan. Expect a re-rating of asset-light/asset-heavy logistics peers — and read-throughs to Omni Logistics' large-customer concentration risk across third-party freight platforms.

items.title: Trump sets July 4 deadline for EU to ratify Turnberry trade deal — threatens tariffs jump to 'much higher' levels with 25% on EU autos and trucks
items.summary: Trump's Truth Social post gives the EU until July 4 to ratify the Turnberry trade deal or tariffs 'immediately jump to much higher levels'; auto duties threatened to rise from 15% to 25%.

explainer.tagline: Trump gives the EU 57 days to ratify the Turnberry trade deal or watch tariffs spike — autos in the crosshairs at 25%.
explainer.whatIsIt: President Trump posted on Truth Social that the EU has until July 4, 2026 — the U.S. 250th birthday — to finalize last summer's Turnberry trade agreement. If the bloc fails to ratify, Trump says tariffs will 'immediately jump to much higher levels,' with EU cars and trucks specifically threatened to climb from the current 15% all-inclusive rate to 25%.
explainer.howItWorks: Under the deal struck in Turnberry, Scotland in 2025, the EU agreed to eliminate remaining tariffs on U.S. goods while the U.S. applied a flat 15% on most EU products. The European Parliament and member states are still negotiating enabling legislation. Wednesday's talks failed; the next round resumes May 19. The main sticking point is whether to include safeguards against a unilateral U.S. breach. Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said 'good progress is being made towards tariff reduction by early July.'
explainer.whyItMatters: EU autos are the most exposed: Germany, Italy and Sweden combined ship roughly $50B+ of vehicles to the U.S. each year. A jump to 25% would mark a return to the pre-Turnberry posture and pressure BMW, Mercedes-Benz, Volkswagen, Stellantis and Volvo Cars. The deadline narrows the window for European legislators and reintroduces tariff-headline risk for the euro and Stoxx 600 industrials — categories that had largely faded from front-page concern after the Turnberry framework was announced.
explainer.forWho: Macro traders, EU equity desks, auto analysts

items.title: Ubiquiti Q3 FY26 — non-GAAP EPS $3.88 misses ~$4.29 est, revenue $788.2M (+18.7% Y/Y but -3.3% Q/Q); UI gaps -9.1% on top-line miss and FCC router-ban margin worry
items.summary: Ubiquiti's Q3 FY26 non-GAAP EPS $3.88 missed Street near $4.29 and revenue $788.2M missed ~$815M, the first sequential decline in five quarters. Shares gapped down ~9% to $842.10 on a 70x P/E and FCC router-ban margin worries.

explainer.tagline: Niche networking-gear maker breaks its top-line streak — sequential decline plus a regulatory overhang trip a 70x P/E.
explainer.whatIsIt: Ubiquiti, the prosumer networking-gear maker known for UniFi access points and routers, posted Q3 fiscal-2026 results on May 8. Both EPS and revenue missed Street, and revenue declined 3.3% sequentially even as it climbed 18.7% Y/Y.
explainer.howItWorks: Revenue $788.2M missed the ~$815M consensus while non-GAAP EPS of $3.88 fell short of the ~$4.29 estimate. GAAP gross margin held at 47.0% (+1.1pp Q/Q) and GAAP EPS of $3.86 was still +29.5% Y/Y. Management noted all outstanding debt was repaid by the end of March, and the board declared a $0.80/share dividend payable May 26. No forward guidance was issued.
explainer.whyItMatters: UI is one of the higher-multiple networking names trading at roughly 70x earnings, leaving little room for a top-line miss. Investors are also weighing the FCC's proposed ban on certain foreign-made routers as a possible margin headwind. Stock gapped down to $842.10, locking in a -9.1% session and -17.4% week. No 13F-style demand catalyst is in sight before fiscal Q4.
explainer.forWho: small/mid-cap networking traders, FCC regulatory watchers

items.title: Fluor Q1 2026 — adj EPS $0.14 misses $0.62 est on $96M Mission Solutions ruling and mining cost growth; FY26 EBITDA guide trimmed to $525–$560M; FLR -5.8% to $51.08
items.summary: Fluor missed Q1 by a wide margin — adj EPS $0.14 vs $0.62 est on a $96M court ruling and mining-project cost growth — and trimmed the FY26 adj EBITDA top-end. Shares closed -5.8% as the company kept its $1.4B 2026 buyback target.

explainer.tagline: Engineering & construction giant misses Q1 by ~78% on EPS, narrows FY26 EBITDA guide as mining cost growth and a $96M court ruling bite.
explainer.whatIsIt: Fluor, a Texas-based engineering, procurement and construction firm, reported Q1 2026 on May 8 before market open. Adjusted EPS of $0.14 came in well below the $0.62 consensus, with revenue of $3.66B also light vs the ~$3.93B Street view.
explainer.howItWorks: Mission Solutions swung to a $71M loss from a $5M profit on a $96M court-ruling charge, while Urban Solutions saw $6M profit (vs $70M) on a $37M mining-project cost increase. Energy Solutions grew to $74M profit on favorable closeouts. The company narrowed the FY26 adj EBITDA top-end to $525–$560M from $525–$585M, citing Q1 cost growth and Middle East-related project delays. Q1 buyback hit $516M and the FY26 buyback target stays at $1.4B; backlog of $25.7B is 82% reimbursable.
explainer.whyItMatters: FLR is a barometer for big-ticket EPC project execution; the magnitude of the miss and the mention of geopolitical delays add to a string of project-charge surprises across the sector. The FY26 EBITDA guide cut is modest in dollar terms but signals visibility issues that pressured the multiple.
