items.title: Trump-brokered Russia-Ukraine 3-day ceasefire (May 9-11) takes hold over Victory Day weekend; both sides exchange ~1,000 POWs each but trade accusations of violations within hours
items.summary: Trump announces 72h Russia-Ukraine ceasefire May 9-11 with 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner swap; Putin holds scaled-back Victory Day parade in Moscow as both sides immediately accuse each other of violations.

explainer.tagline: First Trump-brokered pause in the Russia-Ukraine war coincides with Putin's scaled-back Victory Day parade — already shaky.
explainer.whatIsIt: U.S. President Donald Trump announced Friday May 8 on Truth Social that Russia and Ukraine had agreed to a 72-hour ceasefire running Saturday May 9 through Monday May 11, paired with a 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner exchange. The window deliberately covers Russia's Victory Day commemorations on May 9. Both Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy (on X) and Kremlin foreign-affairs adviser Yuri Ushakov confirmed the agreement.
explainer.howItWorks: The deal is verbal-only — no formal document, no neutral monitors, no defined penalty for violations. Trump described it as 'the beginning of the end of a very long, deadly, and hard fought War'. Putin used his parade speech to declare 'Victory has always been and will be ours' and that the conflict is 'heading to an end but it's still a serious matter'. The Moscow parade itself was sharply downsized — no tanks or missiles for the first time in nearly 20 years, only a jet flyover and infantry, with North Korean troops marching for the first time. Within hours, Russia and Ukraine were already accusing each other of breaking the truce.
explainer.whyItMatters: Markets closed Friday before the announcement, so the first read comes from Sunday-night futures. Defense primes (LMT, RTX, NOC, GD), European defense names (Rheinmetall, BAE, Saab), wheat (ZW=F), gas (TTF) and Brent crude all carry a 'peace premium/discount' that could repress on any sign of durable de-escalation — but the speed of mutual violation accusations argues against pricing-in much. The bigger trade is what doesn't happen: no formal framework, no monitors, no hint of border lines. Sequenced behind the parallel U.S.-Iran MOU talks, this is the second weekend in a row Trump has brokered a fragile geopolitical pause.

image.alt: Russian troops march in scaled-back Victory Day parade on Moscow's Red Square, May 9 2026

items.title: China April 2026 trade — exports +14.1% Y/Y in USD beat 7.9% est, imports +25.3% beat 15.2%; surplus widens to $84.8B
items.summary: China's April exports jumped 14.1% Y/Y in USD terms, sharply outpacing March's +2.5% and the +7.9% Reuters poll. Imports surged 25.3% Y/Y vs +15.2% est. Trade surplus widened to $84.8B from $51.13B in March. GACC released the data Saturday ahead of the May 14-15 Trump-Xi summit.

explainer.tagline: China April exports jump 14.1% Y/Y in USD, blowing past 7.9% consensus as factories race to fulfill orders ahead of Trump-Xi summit.
explainer.whatIsIt: The General Administration of Customs (GACC) released April trade data Saturday morning Beijing time. Exports rose 14.1% Y/Y in USD terms — well above the 7.9% Reuters poll consensus and a sharp rebound from March's 2.5%. Imports surged 25.3% Y/Y vs 15.2% est.
explainer.howItWorks: Trade surplus widened to $84.8B from $51.13B in March. Exports to the U.S. swung from -26.5% in March to +11.3% in April, suggesting front-loading by importers ahead of any Trump-Xi summit outcome. ING's Lynn Song flagged solid external demand in semiconductors and autos.
explainer.whyItMatters: Strong April reflects factories racing to deliver overseas orders amid Iran-war energy worries — buyers stockpiling components. The data lands days before the May 14-15 Trump-Xi summit in Beijing, where tariffs and rare earths are on the agenda. Outsized imports growth is read as a sign of stronger China domestic demand.

items.title: Cerebras (CBRS) raises IPO range to $150–$160 (from $115–$125) on >20× demand; deal up to ~$4.8B with pricing May 13
items.summary: Reuters: Cerebras lifts Nasdaq IPO range to $150–$160/share (from a May 8 bump to $125–$135 off the original $115–$125 filed May 4) and lifts share count to 30M from 28M. Books still >20× covered; pricing eyed May 13.

explainer.tagline: Cerebras lifts its IPO range to $150–$160 — a second bump in three days — as demand stays >20× covered.
explainer.whatIsIt: Cerebras Systems, the AI-chip rival to NVIDIA whose customers include OpenAI and Amazon, has raised the price range on its Nasdaq IPO to $150–$160 per share, up from $125–$135 set May 8 and the original $115–$125 in the May 4 amended S-1. The company also lifted the share count to 30M from 28M.
explainer.howItWorks: At the new $150–$160 range with 30M shares, the deal raises ~$4.5–$4.8B — versus the initial ~$3.5B target. Customers include Amazon and OpenAI; G42 — the UAE AI firm whose ties forced the company to scrap its 2024 IPO attempt — was previously its biggest revenue source. A successful $150-plus print sets a fresh high-water mark for AI-infrastructure capital-markets demand outside NVIDIA.

items.title: Moderna +~14% to two-month high after confirming early-stage hantavirus vaccine work amid MV Hondius outbreak
items.summary: MRNA jumps ~14% Friday after Bloomberg reports Moderna has been running early-stage mRNA hantavirus vaccine research with the U.S. Army and Korea University; trigger is a fatal cruise-ship cluster aboard MV Hondius.

explainer.tagline: Bloomberg story that Moderna has quiet hantavirus vaccine work in flight sends MRNA to a two-month high.
explainer.whatIsIt: Moderna confirmed it has been running early-stage mRNA-based hantavirus vaccine research with the U.S. Army Medical Research Institute of Infectious Diseases, plus a separate 2023 program with the Vaccine Innovation Center at Korea University College of Medicine. The disclosure followed a Bloomberg story published Friday.
explainer.howItWorks: MRNA jumped roughly 14% on the day to a two-month high on volume of ~12M shares — about double the 25-day average of 5.77M. The trigger was renewed public attention on the Dutch-flagged cruise ship MV Hondius, where a hantavirus cluster has caused at least three deaths and prompted a WHO disclosure. No hantavirus vaccine is approved anywhere globally, and human clinical trials for Moderna's candidates have not yet begun.
explainer.whyItMatters: The move reframes Moderna's near-term mRNA pipeline beyond COVID and seasonal flu at a moment when investors have been hunting any non-COVID demand catalyst. The work is preclinical — actual revenue is years away — and the rally is sentiment-led, not contracted-revenue-led.

items.title: Upwork Q1 2026 — revenue $195.5M (+1.4%); cuts ~24% of workforce in $70M restructuring as AI hits low-value gig demand; UPWK -19%
items.summary: Upwork posts Q1 revenue $195.5M (+1.4%) and adj EPS $0.35; cuts ~24% of staff (~145 roles) for ~$70M annual savings; raises FY26 EBITDA guide but Q2 outlook soft. UPWK -19% Thursday.

explainer.tagline: Upwork prints in line on Q1 but cuts ~145 jobs as AI displaces lower-value gig demand; UPWK -19%.
explainer.whatIsIt: Upwork reported Q1 2026 revenue of $195.5M (+1.4% Y/Y), GAAP diluted EPS $0.24 and adj diluted EPS $0.35 — beating bottom-line consensus. Alongside, the company laid out a restructuring plan that cuts ~24% of its workforce, roughly 145 of ~600 employees.
explainer.howItWorks: GSV came in at $987.1M, approximately flat Y/Y, but AI-related GSV grew >40% Y/Y to over $300M. Upwork expects $16–$23M of pre-tax restructuring charges (primarily severance, mostly cash, mostly recognized in Q2 2026) and is targeting ~$70M of annualized savings. FY26 revenue guide is $760–$790M; adj EBITDA guide rises to $250–$260M and adj EPS to $1.50–$1.55.
explainer.whyItMatters: CEO Hayden Brown explicitly attributed the cuts to AI: lower-value freelance work is being absorbed by AI tools, while larger Business Plus and Lifted segments grow. The print and the cuts crystallize the AI-displacement debate at the demand-side end of the labor market — UPWK -19.3% on the day shows the market reading the Q2 deceleration, not the EBITDA raise.

items.title: Gilead Q1 2026 — adj EPS $2.03 beats $1.90 est, raises FY26 sales guide to $30.0–$30.4B; FY EPS guide cut to ($1.05)–($0.65) on $11.5B M&A IPR&D charges; GILD -1.6% AH
items.summary: Gilead Q1 product sales $6.95B (+5%); HIV +10% to $5.0B with Biktarvy $3.36B and Descovy $807M (+38%); Yeztugo PrEP launch $166M with FY guide raised to ~$1B. FY26 product sales guide raised by $400M; non-GAAP EPS guide cut $9.50 on Arcellx/Ouro/Tubulis acquisition charges.

explainer.tagline: HIV-driven Q1 beat and a $400M sales-guide bump, undone for now by $11.5B in deal-related EPS charges.
explainer.whatIsIt: Gilead reported Q1 2026 product sales of $6.95B (+5% Y/Y) and non-GAAP diluted EPS of $2.03 vs $1.90 est. The HIV franchise grew 10% to $5.03B led by Biktarvy ($3.36B, +7%) and Descovy ($807M, +38%, with US PrEP +87%). New PrEP product Yeztugo posted $166M in its first full quarter and is now guided to ~$1B for the year.
explainer.howItWorks: Management raised FY26 product-sales guidance to $30.0–$30.4B (from $29.6–$30.0B) and lifted FY26 HIV growth to ~8% from ~6%. But it cut FY26 non-GAAP diluted EPS to a range of $(1.05)–$(0.65) — versus prior $8.45–$8.85 — citing roughly $11.5B in upfront acquired IPR&D plus financing costs from the Arcellx, Ouro Medicines and Tubulis transactions, equating to about $9.50/share of headwind. Q1 GAAP diluted EPS was $1.61, up from $1.04 a year earlier.
explainer.whyItMatters: GILD slid ~1.6% in after-hours and continued lower into Friday's session as investors weighed the optical EPS hit against an underlying step-up in operating performance. The print confirms PrEP/Yeztugo and HIV are running ahead of plan, but it also crystallizes how aggressively Gilead is leaning into oncology and autoimmune via M&A — a profile shift that mirrors Pfizer's post-Seagen pattern.

items.title: Reportedly: Apollo + Blackstone weigh ~$35B private-credit financing for Broadcom AI chip build — would rank among largest-ever private credit deals; AVGO +3.8%
items.summary: Bloomberg reports Apollo and Blackstone are in talks with Broadcom on a ~$35B private-credit package to fund AI-chip development. AVGO closed +3.8% (peaked +5%); negotiations ongoing per people familiar.

items.title: U.S. consumer sentiment hits record low — U-Michigan May prelim 48.2 (lowest since 1952); gas prices and tariffs cited by ~63% of respondents
items.org: University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers
items.summary: U-Michigan's preliminary May 2026 Index of Consumer Sentiment fell to 48.2 from April's 49.8, missing the 49.5 estimate and marking the lowest reading in the survey's 74-year history. ~33% cited gas prices, ~30% cited tariffs.

explainer.tagline: U-Michigan's May prelim consumer sentiment falls to 48.2 — the lowest reading in the survey's 74-year history.
explainer.whatIsIt: The University of Michigan's monthly Surveys of Consumers, released Friday May 8 2026, posted a preliminary May Index of Consumer Sentiment of 48.2, down 3.2% from April's final 49.8 and below the 49.5 consensus estimate. The Current Economic Conditions sub-index plunged 9% month-over-month to 47.8, while the Expectations sub-index ticked slightly higher to 48.5.
explainer.howItWorks: ICS surveys roughly 600 U.S. households each month on personal finances, business conditions and buying intentions. The 48.2 print marks the lowest in the data series going back to November 1952 — below readings from the 2008 financial crisis and the 1980 stagflation cycle. Director Joanne Hsu noted about one-third of respondents spontaneously mentioned gasoline prices and about 30% mentioned tariffs as top concerns. One-year inflation expectations moderated to 4.5% from 4.7%; long-run expectations eased to 3.4% from 3.5%.
explainer.whyItMatters: Sentiment is a leading indicator for retail and discretionary spending; sub-50 readings have historically aligned with recession periods. The print stands in stark contrast to the same day's strong April payrolls report (+115K) and the S&P 500 / Nasdaq record closes — equities shrugged it off, focusing on the labor data, but Fed officials track sentiment when assessing the consumer side of the dual mandate. With Iran-conflict-driven gasoline prices still elevated and tariff overhang unresolved, follow-through into hard data prints (retail sales, PCE) would pressure consumer-discretionary names.

items.title: Lime (Neutron Holdings) files S-1 for Nasdaq IPO under 'LIME' — flags going-concern risk on $846M debt due in 12 months
items.summary: Uber-backed e-scooter and e-bike platform Lime files for an estimated $250M Nasdaq IPO. Revenue grew to $886.7M in 2025 but the prospectus warns of substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern.

explainer.tagline: Uber-backed micromobility platform files to go public with a going-concern warning attached.
explainer.whatIsIt: Neutron Holdings, parent of e-scooter and e-bike rental company Lime, filed an S-1 to list on Nasdaq under the ticker LIME. The filing — preceded by a confidential submission in October 2025 — pegs the deal at roughly $250M, led by Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Jefferies, Evercore ISI, Citizens JMP and KeyBanc.
explainer.howItWorks: Lime grew gross revenue from $686.6M in 2024 to $886.7M in 2025 and produced $104M of free cash flow last year, but recorded a $59.3M net loss. As of March 31, 2026 it held $261M of cash against ~$1B of current liabilities — including roughly $846M of debt due within 12 months — and the prospectus flags 'substantial doubt' about going-concern unless the IPO or alternative financing closes. About 14.3% of 2025 revenue came through the Uber app integration.
explainer.whyItMatters: First sizeable post-shakeout micromobility IPO and a real-world test of public-market appetite for cash-burning consumer-mobility platforms. The going-concern flag links the deal's success directly to refinancing risk, which makes pricing day a forced-sale event rather than a typical growth-IPO. Uber, which sold its Jump bike unit to Lime in 2020, is the largest outside investor and benefits from a successful listing.

items.title: S&P 500 closes at record 7,398.93 and Nasdaq tops 26,000 for first time — sixth straight weekly gain (longest streak since 2024)
items.summary: S&P 500 +0.84% to 7,398.93, Nasdaq +1.71% to 26,247.08 (first close above 26K), Dow +0.02% to 49,609.16. Both major indexes notch sixth straight weekly gain — longest run since October 2024 — after the April jobs beat and a chip-led rally.

explainer.tagline: S&P 500 and Nasdaq close at fresh records as AI/chip rally and a hot April jobs print extend the longest weekly winning streak since 2024.
explainer.whatIsIt: U.S. equity benchmarks closed Friday at all-time highs to cap a sixth straight weekly gain. The S&P 500 added 0.84% to 7,398.93, the Nasdaq Composite jumped 1.71% to 26,247.08 — its first close above 26,000 — and the Dow eked out 12 points to 49,609.16. The Russell 2000 rose 0.76% to 2,861.19.
explainer.howItWorks: On the week the S&P 500 gained 2.3% and the Nasdaq added 4.5%, both posting six straight winning weeks for the first time since October 2024. Friday's drivers: April nonfarm payrolls of +115K nearly doubled the +65K consensus while unemployment held at 4.3%, and a WSJ scoop on Apple/Intel chipmaking talks sent Intel up ~14% and dragged the wider semi complex higher (Micron +15.5%, Rocket Lab +34%). Cloudflare was the worst S&P performer at -23.6% after its Q1 layoff announcement.
explainer.whyItMatters: The combination of resilient labor data, cooling Iran-driven oil pressure and an AI capex theme that has now lifted AMD past $700B and Micron past $700B in market cap is producing the broadest U.S. equity rally since 2024. Six-week winning streaks are rare and tend to be followed by single-digit pullbacks; macro desks will watch Tuesday's CPI print as the next test of the soft-landing trade.
explainer.forWho: macro and index traders, asset allocators tracking risk-on regimes

items.title: Fidelity National Information Services Q1 2026 — adj EPS $1.36 beats $1.30 est, Banking Solutions +45%; FIS drops ~6% as buybacks and tuck-ins paused to hit 2.8x leverage
items.summary: FIS Q1 GAAP revenue $3.295B (+30% Y/Y, +6.5% pro forma), adj EBITDA +36% to $1.30B, adj EPS $1.36 beats $1.30. Banking Solutions revenue $2.374B (+45% GAAP / +7.7% pro forma); FCF +111% to $474M. Buybacks and M&A paused to hit 2.8x gross leverage.

metrics.FY26 guide: Reiterated; pro forma rev +5.1–5.7%, adj EPS $6.22–$6.32

explainer.tagline: Strong segment growth and a 111% free cash flow surge get overshadowed by a guide-reiteration, soft Q2 outlook and management's deleveraging pause.
explainer.whatIsIt: FIS posted Q1 2026 results before the open Friday. Adjusted EPS of $1.36 topped consensus by ~$0.06 and adjusted EBITDA grew 36% to $1.304B with margin expanding ~290bp to 39.6%. Banking Solutions led the print, with revenue up 45% on a GAAP basis (and 7.7% pro forma) to $2.374B and segment EBITDA margin reaching 43.7%. Capital Market Solutions revenue grew 5% GAAP / 2.9% pro forma to $823M.
explainer.howItWorks: GAAP top-line growth is flattered by the prior-year comp, which excludes the Worldpay business that has since been folded back into FIS's revenue base — pro forma revenue growth was 6.5%. Management has temporarily paused share repurchases and tuck-in M&A to accelerate deleveraging toward a 2.8x gross-leverage target; total debt at quarter end was $21.1B.

items.title: Artivion Q1 2026 — revenue $116.3M (+18%), adj EBITDA +26% to $22.1M, but FY26 guide trimmed; exercises option to acquire Endospan for $175M
items.summary: Q1 revenue $116.3M (+18% reported, +12% cc), adj EBITDA $22.1M (+26%); but FY26 revenue guide trimmed to $480–$496M and EBITDA to $100–$107M on US AMDS and international weakness. AORT also exercises the Endospan option for $175M cash + up to $200M earn-outs.

explainer.tagline: Solid Q1 with stent grafts +21% and On-X +20%, but a softer FY guide and a $175M Endospan close knock AORT down hard.
explainer.whatIsIt: Artivion, the cardiac- and aortic-surgery medtech, reported Q1 2026 revenue of $116.3M (+18% GAAP, +12% constant currency) with adjusted EBITDA up 26% to $22.1M. Net income was $1.4M ($0.03/sh GAAP); non-GAAP EPS of $0.08 beat the Street's $0.06 print.
explainer.howItWorks: Product growth was led by stent grafts (+21%), On-X mechanical valves (+20%) and preservation services (+23%); BioGlue grew 4%. Despite the beat, management cut FY26 revenue guide to $480–$496M (from $486–$504M) and adjusted EBITDA to $100–$107M (from $105–$110M), citing US AMDS starter-set softness, weakness in Middle East stent-graft channels, supply chain bottlenecks and a 23% Latin America revenue decline. Separately, Artivion exercised its option to acquire Endospan now that the FDA PMA-approved NEXUS Aortic Arch System is in hand: base price $175M (net ~$135M after loan offsets), plus up to $200M of contingent consideration tied to future NEXUS performance, expected to close in Q2 2026.
explainer.whyItMatters: Two stories collide on one print — a clean operational quarter and a real guidance reset that the market punished hard, with shares dropping ~30% intraday. The Endospan exercise locks in NEXUS, the only PMA-approved aortic arch system, and signals AORT is willing to lever up the existing term loan to secure long-cycle stent-graft growth even as near-term execution slips.

items.title: Toyota FY26 — record ¥50.7T revenue but op income -21.5% on ¥1.38T US tariff hit; FY27 guide cuts another 20% to ¥3T
items.summary: Toyota's fiscal 2026 (Apr 2025–Mar 2026) printed record net revenue of ¥50.7T and 9.6M global units, but op income fell 21.5% to ¥3.77T as US tariffs cost ¥1.38T and dragged North America to a ¥298.6B loss.

explainer.tagline: Record sales year, but the US tariff bill turned Toyota's North American operation into a $2B loss.
explainer.whatIsIt: Toyota Motor Corporation reported full-year FY2026 results on May 8 (covering April 2025–March 2026). Vehicle sales hit a record 9.595M units, revenue rose 5.5% to ¥50.68T, but operating income fell 21.5% to ¥3.77T and Q4 op income missed badly at ¥569.4B vs ¥813.3B Street estimate.
explainer.howItWorks: Toyota disclosed a ¥1.38T (~$9B) operating-income hit from US tariffs in FY26, plus a ¥2.03T currency drag. North America (excluding interest-rate-swap valuation effects) swung from a ¥108.8B profit to a ¥298.6B (~$2.0B) operating loss even though regional volumes grew 8.5%. Europe and Asia held positive, but Q4 group volume slipped to 2.29M units. For FY27 (ending March 2027), Toyota guides operating income to ¥3.0T — another roughly 20% reduction — including an additional ¥670B tariff hit on a 150 yen/USD assumption.
explainer.whyItMatters: Toyota is the world's largest automaker by units, and this is the cleanest read yet on how Section 232/IEEPA-era US auto tariffs flow through a Japanese OEM's P&L. Tokyo shares closed -2.18% at ¥2,913. The tariff regime here is the same one the US Court of International Trade ruled unlawful for the 10% Section 122 duties this week — but auto tariffs were on a separate authority and are not affected by that ruling, so the FY27 guide builds them in. Read-through to other Japanese/Korean OEMs (Honda, Nissan, Hyundai) and US-listed parts suppliers (BWA, MGA, ALV).

items.title: AWS US-East-1 thermal event takes Coinbase offline ~7 hours, hits FanDuel and CME — single-AZ outage cascades
items.summary: AWS Northern Virginia data center overheated late May 7, knocking out the use1-az4 availability zone. Coinbase trading was down ~7 hours; FanDuel and CME Group also hit. Several services still impaired into May 8.

explainer.tagline: An overheated data hall in northern Virginia took down a slice of the cloud — and the consumer apps that quietly run on it.
explainer.whatIsIt: On May 7 around 4:20 PM PDT, AWS reported a thermal event in a single availability zone (use1-az4) of its US-East-1 region in Northern Virginia. EC2 instances and EBS volumes lost power, and a slate of higher-level services degraded as failover did not behave as designed. Recovery extended into May 8 as cooling capacity was rebuilt incrementally.
explainer.howItWorks: Power to the affected hardware was restored by 9:12 PM PDT, but services like ElastiCache, MSK (Kafka) and OpenSearch remained impaired hours later. Coinbase users were locked out of trades, balance checks and withdrawals for roughly seven hours; the exchange noted custody systems were unaffected. FanDuel users could not cash out mid-game; CME Group's trading platform was also disrupted.
explainer.whyItMatters: It is the second material AWS-driven Coinbase outage since October 2025 and lands the same day Coinbase reported a $394M Q1 GAAP loss. The pattern keeps reopening the question of single-region concentration risk for crypto exchanges, sportsbooks and U.S. derivatives venues that all sit on the same Virginia footprint.
explainer.forWho: infra teams, crypto traders, sportsbook bettors, exchange-risk desks

items.title: U.S. Court of International Trade rules Trump's 10% global Section 122 tariffs unlawful in 2-1 panel decision
items.summary: A three-judge CIT panel ruled 2-1 that Trump exceeded his authority under Section 122 of the Trade Act when he imposed the February 2026 10% global tariff. Permanent injunction applies only to the named plaintiffs.

explainer.tagline: CIT strikes down Trump's second-attempt 10% global tariffs as unlawful under Section 122 — but only the three plaintiffs get relief.
explainer.whatIsIt: The U.S. Court of International Trade ruled 2-1 in Burlap and Barrel, Inc. v. Trump that the 10% global import surcharge Trump imposed in February 2026 — after the Supreme Court struck down his earlier IEEPA-based 'Liberation Day' tariffs — was unlawful. The court held the administration could not invoke Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 because that statute requires a balance-of-payments deficit, not a goods trade deficit.
explainer.howItWorks: A three-judge CIT panel issued a 53-page opinion finding the administration conflated balance-of-payments deficits with trade deficits to justify the tariffs. The permanent injunction was narrow: tariffs cannot be charged on imports of the three plaintiffs (Burlap and Barrel, Basic Fun, the State of Washington) and refunds with interest are owed on duties already paid by them. Claims by 23 other states were dismissed for lack of standing. The administration is expected to appeal to the Federal Circuit and ultimately the Supreme Court.
explainer.whyItMatters: Two of Trump's two tariff legal vehicles have now been struck down — IEEPA at the Supreme Court in February and Section 122 at the CIT now. With CBP CAPE refunds already starting on the IEEPA tariffs, importers and corporates are running scenarios for either a third legal theory or a Federal Circuit reversal. The narrow injunction means most importers still pay the 10% for now, but the precedent makes a broader injunction or class-wide refund more likely if the appeal fails.
explainer.forWho: macro traders, importers, retailers, supply-chain analysts

items.title: Wendy's Q1 2026 — global same-store sales -6.8% (US -7.8%) and 146 net store closures, but EPS/EBITDA top lowered bar; WEN +7.6%
items.summary: Wendy's posts Q1 revenue $540.6M (+3.3%) and adj EPS $0.12 above the lowered Street bar, but US same-store sales fell 7.8% and the chain shrunk by 146 restaurants in the quarter; WEN bounces 7.6% off a 52-week low.

explainer.tagline: Wendy's beats lowered estimates but US comps -7.8% and 146 net store closures lay bare the depth of the QSR's turnaround challenge.
explainer.whatIsIt: Wendy's reported Q1 2026 results on May 8 with adjusted EPS of $0.12 beating the $0.10 consensus on revenue of $540.6 million (+3.3% Y/Y, vs $518.1M est). Adjusted EBITDA of $111.3M topped the $102.8M Street view but fell 10.6% Y/Y. Adj EPS itself dropped from $0.20 a year ago.
explainer.howItWorks: Global same-store sales declined 6.8%, with the US off 7.8% and international down 0.4%; systemwide sales fell 5.5% globally to $3.2B. Interim CEO Ken Cook reaffirmed FY26 guide for roughly flat systemwide sales, adj EBITDA $460–480M, adj EPS $0.56–0.60 and FCF $190–205M.

items.title: U.S. April 2026 jobs report — payrolls +115K beat 62K est, unemployment steady at 4.3%, March revised up to +185K
items.summary: Nonfarm payrolls rose 115,000 in April, nearly double the 62K LSEG consensus. Unemployment held at 4.3%. February revised down 23K to -156K; March revised up 7K to +185K. Healthcare, transport and retail led gains; federal employment fell.

explainer.tagline: April payrolls almost double consensus and March revised higher — labor market still resilient as Fed weighs cuts.
explainer.whatIsIt: The Bureau of Labor Statistics released the April 2026 Employment Situation report on Friday, May 8 at 8:30 a.m. ET. It is the first jobs print since the FOMC's late-April hold, and the input most watched into the June 16–17 meeting.
explainer.howItWorks: Total nonfarm payrolls rose 115,000 in April vs the 62,000 LSEG / 65,000 FactSet consensus. The unemployment rate was unchanged at 4.3%. February was revised down 23K to -156K and March revised up 7K to +185K, leaving the two-month net 16K lower. Health care, transportation and warehousing, and retail trade were the largest contributors; federal government payrolls continued to fall. Average workweek ticked up 0.1 hr to 34.3.
explainer.whyItMatters: Back-to-back monthly gains for the first time in roughly a year argue against the recession framing some had built around February's negative print. With unemployment steady, the cooling-but-resilient narrative holds: rates desks priced odds of a June cut lower, and longer-end Treasury yields had spent the week hovering around the 5% line on the 30-year. Growth-sensitive equities and small caps benefit if prints keep landing in the 'soft landing' window.

items.summary: BD posts Q2 FY26 revenue $4.71B (+5.2%), adj EPS $2.90 (+3.9%); raises FY26 adj EPS guide to $12.52–$12.72; executes $2B accelerated buyback and retires $2.1B debt — BDX rallies ~5.7%.

explainer.tagline: BD beats on EPS, raises FY26 guide, and follows through with a $2B accelerated buyback.
explainer.whatIsIt: Becton Dickinson reported fiscal Q2 2026 results on May 7. Revenue was $4.714B (+5.2% reported, +2.6% FXN), and adj EPS was $2.90, ahead of the $2.78 consensus. GAAP EPS was -$0.13 on restructuring/separation costs.
explainer.howItWorks: Adjusted operating margin of 24.2% expanded from the prior year. Management raised FY26 adj EPS guide to $12.52–$12.72 from the prior $12.35–$12.65 range, citing first-half performance and improved second-half visibility. Capital actions: a $2.0B accelerated share repurchase executed in the quarter and ~$2.1B of debt retired.
explainer.whyItMatters: BD is one of the larger medtech bellwethers; a beat-and-raise paired with a sizable buyback supports the medtech recovery narrative going into the BD-Solventum spin work. Read-throughs to large-cap diversified medtech (MDT, ABT, SYK) and to medical-supplies pricing.

items.title: Insulet Q1 2026 — revenue $761.7M (+33.9%), adj EPS $1.42 beats $1.19 est; FY26 guide raised but PODD -10% on full-year EPS read
items.summary: Insulet posts Q1 revenue $761.7M (+33.9%, +30.1% cc) and adj EPS $1.42 vs $1.19 est, lifting FY26 revenue growth to 21–23% cc; PODD still falls ~10% as full-year EPS implication trails consensus.

explainer.tagline: Insulet beats and raises but PODD slides ~10% as the implied FY EPS lands below the Street.
explainer.whatIsIt: Insulet reported Q1 2026 results on May 6. Revenue rose 33.9% to $761.7M with Omnipod revenue +36.9% to $758.4M; international Omnipod was +59.4%. Adjusted EPS of $1.42 beat the $1.19 consensus.
explainer.howItWorks: Q1 GAAP EPS was $1.30 versus $0.50 a year ago. Insulet raised FY26 revenue growth guidance to 21–23% in constant currency (from 20–22%) and reiterated ~100bp adj operating margin expansion. The company also flagged enrollment of the first patient in the EVOLVE pivotal trial for its fully closed-loop AID system in type-2 diabetes, intended to support a planned 2027 510(k) filing.
explainer.whyItMatters: PODD is the dominant patch-pump platform and a key read on diabetes-tech adoption versus Tandem and Medtronic. The 10% drop after a beat shows how tightly the stock is priced for the FY EPS line — BTIG cut its PT from $320 to $260 while keeping a Buy. Read-throughs to TNDM, MDT, DXCM.

items.title: Lyft Q1 2026 — gross bookings $4.9B (+19%), revenue $1.65B (+14%), adj EBITDA $132.8M (+25%); Q2 GB guide $5.30–$5.43B
items.summary: Lyft Q1 gross bookings $4.9B (+19%), revenue $1.65B (+14%), 28.3M active riders (+17%); adj EBITDA $132.8M (+25%); Q2 guide gross bookings $5.30–$5.43B and adj EBITDA $160–$180M.

explainer.tagline: Lyft delivers a record FCF quarter and 19% gross-bookings growth — but a small EPS miss caps the move.
explainer.whatIsIt: Lyft reported Q1 2026 results on May 7. Gross bookings grew 19% year-over-year to $4.9B, revenue rose 14% to $1.65B, and active riders climbed 17% to 28.3M. Adjusted EBITDA was $132.8M, up 25%, and the company posted its highest-ever trailing-twelve-month free cash flow.
explainer.howItWorks: Diluted EPS of $0.04 was a small miss versus consensus near $0.06. For Q2, Lyft guided gross bookings to $5.30–$5.43B (18–21% growth) and adjusted EBITDA to $160–$180M, both above Street expectations.
explainer.whyItMatters: Lyft is the cleanest pure-play comparable to Uber's mobility segment. Q1 2026 strength suggests rideshare demand is holding up; the gap between Lyft's operating momentum and its share-price reaction (-3% on the print) frames the rideshare-vs-robotaxi narrative running into Q2.

image.alt: Lyft Q1 2026 financial results banner

items.title: CVS Health Q1 2026 — revenue $100.4B (+6.2%), adj EPS $2.57 vs $2.20 est, raises FY26 EPS guide to $7.30–$7.50; CVS +7%
items.summary: CVS beat across every segment, with Aetna driving over $1B of Y/Y operating-income improvement. Management lifted FY26 adj EPS guide to $7.30–$7.50, revenue to ≥$405B and OCF to ≥$9.5B.

explainer.tagline: Aetna's turnaround drives CVS's biggest beat in years and a meaningful guidance raise.
explainer.whatIsIt: CVS Health reported Q1 2026 results that topped expectations across all three segments — Health Care Benefits, Health Services and Pharmacy & Consumer Wellness — and raised every line of full-year guidance.
explainer.howItWorks: Total revenue rose 6.2% Y/Y to $100.4B vs $95.0B est. Adj EPS came in at $2.57 vs $2.20 consensus; GAAP EPS jumped 63% to $2.30. Health Care Benefits adj operating income rose 52.6% to $3.04B as Aetna's medical-cost trends improved. FY26 adj EPS guide raised to $7.30–$7.50 (from $7.00–$7.20), revenue to ≥$405B, and operating cash flow to ≥$9.5B.
explainer.whyItMatters: CVS shares had been a multi-year underperformer on Aetna cost concerns and PBM regulatory overhang. A clean beat with a Y/Y operating-income jump at Aetna and a four-point EPS guide bump is the strongest signal yet that the insurance turnaround is taking hold. Other managed-care names (UNH, ELV, HUM, CI) trade off the same medical-loss-ratio backdrop.

image.alt: CVS Health logo

items.title: Gen Digital FQ4 FY26 — first $5B revenue year, non-GAAP EPS $2.56 (+15%); raises FY27 revenue growth guide to 8–10%; GEN +8% AH
items.summary: Norton/LifeLock parent crossed $5B in annual revenue for the first time as the MoneyLion-led Trust-Based Solutions segment grew 20%+. Management lifted FY27 growth guide to 8–10% and EPS to $2.85–$2.95.

explainer.tagline: Gen's first $5B year — and a step-change in growth — say the MoneyLion bet is paying off.
explainer.whatIsIt: Gen Digital, the consumer-cyber group behind Norton, LifeLock, Avast, Avira and CCleaner, reported FQ4 and FY26 results. The MoneyLion acquisition closed last year boosted reported numbers, but pro forma growth still re-accelerated.
explainer.howItWorks: FY26 revenue $5.00B (+27% reported, +9% pro forma) with Q4 $1.28B (+27% reported). Non-GAAP FY26 EPS $2.56 (+15%); GAAP EPS $1.57 (+53%). Trust-Based Solutions (MoneyLion) grew 20%+; Cyber Safety grew mid-single digits. FY27 guide: revenue $5.325–$5.425B (+8–10%), non-GAAP EPS $2.85–$2.95 (mid-teens growth), with leverage already at the 3x EBITDA target a year ahead of plan.
explainer.whyItMatters: Gen had been a low-growth subscription-cyber name. Lifting the growth guide from mid-single digits to 8–10% and the EPS bracket above the prior consensus $2.78 is the meaningful change — it puts Gen in the same growth conversation as some pure-play cybersecurity peers. MoneyLion turning into a near-$1B run-rate consumer-fintech engine inside Gen is the new bull story.

image.alt: Gen Digital Q4 FY26 results — one-page summary graphic

items.title: JFrog Q1 2026 — revenue $154M (+26%), cloud +50%, raises FY26 cloud guide and adds $300M buyback; FROG +17.5%
items.summary: JFrog reported Q1 2026 revenue of $154M (+26% Y/Y), cloud revenue $78.9M (+50%), non-GAAP EPS $0.27; raised FY26 cloud growth guide to 33-35% and authorized a $300M buyback. Stock jumped 17.5% post-print.

explainer.tagline: JFrog's cloud business crosses 51% of revenue; FY26 cloud growth guide raised to 33-35% with a fresh $300M buyback.
explainer.whatIsIt: JFrog reported Q1 2026 results on May 7. Revenue rose 26% to $154M, cloud revenue jumped 50% to $78.9M and now makes up 51% of the mix for the first time. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.27, beating the ~$0.21 consensus.
explainer.howItWorks: Net dollar retention was 120%, gross retention 97%, and customers spending over $1M annually grew 48% Y/Y to 80. Management raised the FY26 cloud growth target to 33-35%, set the net dollar retention floor at 118%, and authorized a fresh $300M share repurchase. Q2 revenue guide is $154-156M; FY26 revenue guide is $628-632M (~18.5% growth at the midpoint).
explainer.whyItMatters: The print extends a string of strong Q1 software prints (DDOG, FTNT) and reinforces that DevOps spend on AI-era artifact security is still scaling. The buyback signals confidence in cash generation despite continued reinvestment. FROG closed up 17.5% on the day.

image.alt: JFrog Q1 2026 earnings press release cover

items.title: Innodata Q1 2026 — revenue $90.1M (+54%), EPS $0.42; new $51M Big Tech engagement, raises FY26 growth guide to 40%+; INOD +30% AH
items.summary: Innodata posted record Q1 revenue of $90.1M (+54% Y/Y), EPS $0.42 (vs $0.22 prior), adj EBITDA $25M; signed a new $51M-in-2026 Big Tech AI deal and raised FY26 growth guide to ~40%+. INOD spiked from mid-$40s to low $60s in extended trade.

explainer.tagline: Innodata's AI-services run rate accelerates: Q1 revenue +54%, raises FY26 growth target to 40%+, and lands a $51M Big Tech engagement.
explainer.whatIsIt: Innodata, an AI training-data and engineering services provider for hyperscalers, reported Q1 2026 results on May 7. Revenue hit a record $90.1M (+54% Y/Y), net income nearly doubled to $14.9M ($0.42 per diluted share), and adjusted EBITDA rose to $25M (28% margin).
explainer.howItWorks: Management disclosed a new engagement with a leading Big Tech customer expected to add about $51M of 2026 revenue and raised full-year guidance to ~40%+ Y/Y growth, well above prior 'at least 40%' framing. Cash and short-term investments climbed to $117.4M from $82.2M at year-end. Adjusted gross margin expanded as new programs ramped.
explainer.whyItMatters: INOD has become a small-cap proxy for hyperscaler AI training-data spend; Q1 puts the company on track for $360M+ FY26 revenue versus ~$255M in 2025. Shares spiked from the mid-$40s to the low $60s in extended trading, a roughly 30% move, on the deal disclosure and guide raise.

image.alt: Innodata Q1 2026 earnings press release cover

items.title: MercadoLibre Q1 2026 — revenue $8.85B (+49% Y/Y, fastest since 2022) but EPS $8.23 misses $8.83 est; MELI -5.9% AH
items.summary: MercadoLibre reported Q1 net revenue $8.85B (+49% Y/Y, fastest since Q2 2022) topping Street, but EPS $8.23 missed $8.83 est on heavy fintech/credit and 1P investment. Brazil GMV +38%, Mexico +28%; MELI dropped ~5.9% AH.

explainer.tagline: Revenue tops Street with 49% growth — best since 2022 — but credit-card buildout drags margins and EPS misses for a fourth straight quarter.
explainer.whatIsIt: MercadoLibre reported Q1 2026 results on May 7. Net revenue rose 49% Y/Y to $8.85B, the fastest pace since Q2 2022, beating analyst expectations near $8.46B. EPS came in at $8.23 versus $8.83 consensus on heavy fintech and 1P/CBT spend.
explainer.howItWorks: Brazil GMV grew 38% Y/Y with items sold accelerating to +56% after a lower free-shipping threshold; Mexico GMV +28% and Argentina +41%. Mercado Pago issued 2.7M new credit cards, lifting the credit portfolio to ~$14.6B (close to 2x Y/Y); credit-card TPV grew 90% Y/Y. Free shipping, 1P expansion and credit card scaling all weighed on margins.
explainer.whyItMatters: MELI is the LatAm bellwether for both ecommerce and fintech; the revenue acceleration validates investment in shipping and credit, but the fourth straight EPS miss tested patience and sent shares down ~5.9% in after-hours trading. The credit portfolio doubling is a structural growth unlock and a credit-quality watch item.

image.alt: MercadoLibre Q1 2026 results cover

items.title: HubSpot Q1 2026 — revenue $881M (+23%), non-GAAP EPS $2.72 beats; HUBS drops ~12% on Customer Agent price cut, slow Q2 start
items.summary: HubSpot beat on Q1 revenue and EPS and raised FY26 guidance, but the stock fell ~12% post-print as management lowered Customer Agent pricing, shifted to outcome-based pricing for AI agents, and flagged a slow Q2 start.

explainer.tagline: Beat-and-raise wasn't enough — pricing cuts on AI agents and a slow Q2 start sent HUBS down ~12%.
explainer.whatIsIt: HubSpot reported Q1 2026 results after the close on May 7, 2026. Revenue of $881M (+23% Y/Y) and non-GAAP EPS of $2.72 both beat consensus, GAAP swung to a $32.6M profit, and the company raised FY26 revenue and EPS guidance. Despite this, shares dropped ~11.9% post-print to ~$214.86.
explainer.howItWorks: Subscription revenue grew 23% to $862.3M, customers crossed ~300K (+16%), and non-GAAP operating margin expanded ~380bp to 17.8%. FY26 guidance moved up to $3.700–$3.708B revenue (+18%) and $13.04–$13.12 in non-GAAP diluted EPS. The catch: management lowered the price of Customer Agent, moved Customer and Prospecting Agents to outcome-based pricing, introduced 28-day agent free trials, and warned Q2 had a slow start with extended sales cycles.
explainer.whyItMatters: HubSpot has been pitching itself as the agentic CRM, but Wall Street read the pricing changes as a signal that AI agents aren't yet pulling their weight in commercial terms. Read-through to other AI-monetization stories (CRM, NOW, FRSH) and to the broader 'agents are commoditizing' debate. Buyback ramped to $211M in the quarter against a $1.8B cash position.

image.alt: HubSpot Q1 2026 8-K filing cover

items.title: Toast Q1 2026 — revenue $1.63B (+22%), net income doubles to $126M, raises FY26 EBITDA guide to $790–$810M; TOST -10.5%
items.summary: Toast beat on EPS ($0.29 vs $0.16 est), revenue in line at $1.63B (+22%), added ~7K net locations and raised FY26 adj EBITDA guide to $790–$810M; TOST still fell 10.5% on richly-priced expectations and 2026 setup.

explainer.tagline: Strong print, raised guide — but TOST still slid ~10% as the bar was already very high.
explainer.whatIsIt: Toast posted Q1 2026 results after the close on May 7, 2026. Revenue of $1.63B (+22%) was in line with Street, EPS of $0.29 nearly doubled the $0.16 consensus, and FY26 adjusted EBITDA guidance was raised to $790–$810M from $775–$795M. Despite the beat-and-raise, shares dropped ~10.5%.
explainer.howItWorks: Annualized recurring run-rate climbed 26% to $2.2B, gross payment volume reached $51.3B (+22%), and the platform added roughly 7K net new restaurant locations to ~171K total. Adjusted EBITDA rose to $179M from $133M, free cash flow grew to $115M, and the company launched Toast IQ Grow with its first AI agent for restaurants. Net income more than doubled to $126M.
explainer.whyItMatters: Toast had rallied into the print on payments scale-economics and AI optionality, so a clean beat that wasn't a blowout was punished. The ~7K net adds is decelerating versus prior quarters, and Q2 commentary on macro restaurant softness drove most of the move. Read-through to other restaurant tech and SMB payments names like SQ/XYZ, FOUR, and Olo.

image.alt: Toast Q1 2026 8-K filing cover

items.title: Affirm FQ3 2026 — revenue $1.04B (+33%), EPS $0.30 trounces $0.19 est, GMV $11.6B (+35%); raises FY26 revenue guide to $4.175–$4.205B
items.summary: Affirm beat on revenue and EPS, hit $11.6B GMV (+35% Y/Y), grew Affirm Card cardholders to 4.4M, and raised FY26 revenue guidance to $4.175–$4.205B; AH stock barely budged on stretched-valuation concerns.

explainer.tagline: Big beat-and-raise, but the stock had run into the print — flat AH despite GMV +35% and revenue +33%.
explainer.whatIsIt: Affirm reported fiscal Q3 2026 (quarter ended March 31, 2026) on May 7, 2026. Revenue of $1.04B beat the $993M consensus, adjusted EPS of $0.30 trounced the $0.19 estimate, and GMV grew 35% to $11.6B. Management raised the FY26 revenue guide to $4.175–$4.205B and FQ4 to $1.08–$1.11B.
explainer.howItWorks: Active consumers grew 22% to 26.8M, active merchants 47% to 515K, and total transactions 45% to 45M. Affirm Card cardholders reached 4.4M with $2.1B of card GMV in the quarter. Direct-to-consumer GMV grew 48% to $3.7B. CEO Max Levchin said the Affirm consumer is 'financially healthy' with stable credit and an 'unusually supportive funding environment.'
explainer.whyItMatters: Affirm has been the bellwether BNPL/fintech name and the print confirms the model is scaling with stable credit even as the stock has more than doubled off 52-week lows. Modest AH reaction (-0.2% to ~$67.50) reflects valuation rather than results, with read-throughs to PYPL, KLAR, SOFI and the broader 'consumer is fine' macro debate.

image.alt: Affirm FQ3 2026 earnings press release

items.title: Akamai Q1 2026 — revenue $1.07B in line, but $1.8B/7-yr AI cloud commitment from frontier-model provider sends AKAM ~+26%
items.summary: Akamai delivered an in-line print (revenue $1.074B +6%, non-GAAP EPS $1.61) but disclosed the largest customer deal in its history: a leading frontier-model provider committing $1.8B over seven years for Cloud Infrastructure Services. AKAM surged ~26% to a 52-week high.

explainer.tagline: Boring earnings, blockbuster customer disclosure: a frontier-model lab just signed Akamai's largest deal ever.
explainer.whatIsIt: Akamai posted Q1 2026 results after the close on May 7, 2026. Revenue of $1.074B (+6%) and non-GAAP EPS of $1.61 were both effectively in line with Street, but the company separately disclosed that 'a leading U.S.-based frontier model provider' committed $1.8B over seven years for Cloud Infrastructure Services — the largest customer contract in Akamai's history.
explainer.howItWorks: Security revenue grew 11% to $590M, Cloud Infrastructure Services accelerated to +40% Y/Y at $95M, and the legacy Delivery business shrank 7% to $389M. Non-GAAP operating margin was about 26%. Akamai guided Q2 revenue of $1.075–$1.10B and FY26 of $4.445–$4.55B with non-GAAP EPS of $6.40–$7.15. The $1.8B AI deal is a multi-year compute commitment, not a one-time payment.
explainer.whyItMatters: Akamai has been an awkward AI story — a CDN whose core delivery franchise is bleeding to hyperscalers — but the frontier-lab contract reframes the cloud-infrastructure unit as a credible neocloud. AKAM closed up ~26% to ~$147, a 52-week high, with read-throughs to other AI-adjacent infra names like CRWV, NBIS, and APLD.

image.alt: Akamai Technologies Q1 2026 earnings release header

items.title: Block Q1 2026 — adj EPS $0.85 (+52%), record 25% adj op margin and FY26 guide raised; XYZ ~+8% AH despite $309M GAAP loss
items.summary: Block grew gross profit 27% to $2.91B and lifted adjusted EPS 52% to $0.85, with Cash App gross profit up 38% and a record 25% adjusted operating margin. The company raised FY26 guidance and shares climbed ~8% AH despite a $309M GAAP net loss tied to restructuring.

explainer.tagline: Cash App reaccelerated, margins hit a record, and Block raised every line of FY26 guidance — even with a messy GAAP print.
explainer.whatIsIt: Block reported Q1 2026 after the close on May 7, 2026. Net revenue was $6.06B, gross profit grew 27% to $2.91B, and adjusted operating income hit $728M — a record 25% margin — as adjusted diluted EPS rose 52% to $0.85.
explainer.howItWorks: Cash App gross profit rose 38% to $1.91B; Square gross profit grew 9% to $982M. The company recorded a $309M GAAP net loss after $852M of restructuring/other charges and a $173M bitcoin remeasurement loss tied to lower BTC prices. Block raised FY26 guidance to $12.33B in gross profit (+19%), $3.34B adjusted operating income at a 27% margin, and $3.85 adjusted EPS (+62%).
explainer.whyItMatters: After a brutal early-2026, Block's reaccelerating Cash App and record margin print is a meaningful 'fintech is back' signal. The raised FY guide cuts the other way from PayPal's cautious tone earlier in the week, and shares jumped ~8% AH.
explainer.forWho: fintech / payments investors, Cash App and Square watchers

image.alt: Block Q1 2026 8-K filing cover image

items.title: Monster Beverage Q1 2026 — net sales +27% to $2.35B, adj EPS $0.58 beats $0.53 est on international and Strategic Brands strength; MNST +4%
items.summary: Monster Beverage's Q1 2026 net sales jumped 26.9% to $2.35B with adj EPS $0.58 beating the $0.53 consensus. Monster Energy segment grew 27.6%, Strategic Brands +28.9%, and gross margin slipped 150bp to 55.0% on aluminum and freight. MNST +4.2% AH.

explainer.tagline: Monster's energy-drink franchise reaccelerated and Strategic Brands carried the international story, even as gross margin compressed 150bp.
explainer.whatIsIt: Monster Beverage reported Q1 2026 after the close on May 7, 2026. Net sales rose 26.9% to $2.35B and adjusted EPS of $0.58 topped the $0.53 consensus, helped by international and the Strategic Brands portfolio.
explainer.howItWorks: Monster Energy segment sales rose 27.6% to $2.19B; Strategic Brands jumped 28.9% to $126.7M. Gross margin compressed to 55.0% from 56.5% on geographic mix, higher aluminum can costs and freight-in, partly offset by pricing actions.
explainer.whyItMatters: Coming after Celsius posted a record Q1 with margin compression of its own, Monster's print confirms the energy-drink category is still expanding even as inputs bite. The +4.2% AH move puts MNST back near its multi-year highs.

image.alt: Monster Beverage Q1 2026 earnings release header

items.title: Texas Roadhouse Q1 2026 — revenue +12.8% to $1.63B, EPS $1.87 beats, comp sales +7.1%; raises dividend to $0.75 and TXRH +6%
items.summary: Texas Roadhouse grew Q1 revenue 12.8% to $1.63B with comparable sales +7.1% and EPS $1.87, ahead of the $1.79 consensus. The board raised the quarterly dividend to $0.75 from $0.68 and the stock climbed ~6% on the print.

explainer.tagline: Traffic-led comps, a beat on EPS and a dividend raise — casual dining's standout keeps printing in a soft consumer.
explainer.whatIsIt: Texas Roadhouse reported Q1 2026 results on May 7, 2026. Revenue rose 12.8% to $1.63B, comparable restaurant sales grew 7.1% (with a roughly 1.9% April price increase), and diluted EPS climbed to $1.87 from $1.70.
explainer.howItWorks: Net income rose to $123M. Restaurant-level margin slipped to 16.3% from 16.6% Y/Y on labor and commodity inflation. The board approved a $0.75 quarterly dividend (from $0.68), payable June 30 to holders of record June 2. The system ended the quarter with 822 restaurants.
explainer.whyItMatters: While Sweetgreen flagged a -12.8% comp and Whirlpool blamed Iran for soft consumer confidence, Texas Roadhouse continued to take traffic share. The +6% move and dividend hike reinforce TXRH's status as the casual-dining bellwether.

image.alt: Texas Roadhouse Q1 2026 earnings release header

items.title: Planet Fitness Q1 2026 — beats on Q1 (rev $337M, EPS $0.65) but pauses Black Card price hike and cuts FY26 EPS guide to $3.19; PLNT crashes ~30%
items.summary: PLNT beat Q1 (rev +21.9% to $337.2M, EPS $0.65) but slashed 2026 guide and cancelled the planned Black Card price hike after net new member adds fell 36% Y/Y. Stock plunged ~30% to a 52-week low.

explainer.tagline: Strong Q1 print, but the FY guide cut and the cancelled Black Card price hike send PLNT to a 52-week low.
explainer.whatIsIt: Planet Fitness reported Q1 2026 results before the bell on May 7, 2026. The Q1 numbers cleared the Street, but management lowered FY26 guidance and paused a planned national Black Card price increase pending a broader pricing review.
explainer.howItWorks: Q1 revenue rose 21.9% to $337.2M, adjusted EBITDA was $139.9M (+19.5% Y/Y) and adjusted EPS of $0.74 beat the $0.63 consensus. But net new member adds fell 36% Y/Y in the peak January–March sign-up season, with management citing weather, weaker resonance with beginner customers, and competitive pressure in the Sunbelt. FY26 adjusted EPS guide was cut to $3.19 (from a $3.35–$3.38 range), and revenue guide was cut to $1.41B from $1.44B.
explainer.whyItMatters: The Black Card price hike was a key bull-case lever for 2026 EPS, and pulling it forces a rethink of unit economics. PLNT shares fell ~30% to a 52-week low — the steepest drop since 2020 — and the move drags on consumer-discretionary peers exposed to membership-based business models.

image.alt: Planet Fitness 8-K filing cover for Q1 2026 results

items.title: SiTime Q1 2026 — revenue $113.6M (+88% Y/Y), non-GAAP EPS $1.44 vs $1.16 est; AI/data-center timing demand drives Barclays upgrade with $850 PT, SITM +24%
items.summary: SiTime's Q1 revenue jumped 88% to $113.6M, beating $105.5M est. Non-GAAP EPS was $1.44 vs $1.16 est; CED segment grew 158% Y/Y on AI XPUs and 1.6Tb optical modules. Barclays double-upgraded to OW with PT $850, sending SITM +24% on May 7.

explainer.tagline: SiTime's MEMS timing chips ride the AI infrastructure build — and the Q1 print plus a Barclays double-upgrade re-rate the stock.
explainer.whatIsIt: SiTime designs precision MEMS timing solutions used to synchronize signals across AI data center clusters, optical modules, and high-performance compute. The company reported Q1 2026 results after the close on May 6, 2026.
explainer.howItWorks: Revenue grew 88.3% Y/Y to $113.6M, beating the $105.5M consensus. Non-GAAP EPS was $1.44 versus $1.16 estimated; non-GAAP gross margin was 64.5%. The Communications, Enterprise & Data Center (CED) segment grew 158% Y/Y, driven by AI inference XPUs, 1.6Tb optical modules, switches, co-packaged optics, and the new Elite 2 Super-TCXO. The company ended Q1 with $788.7M in cash and short-term investments.
explainer.whyItMatters: SITM is one of the cleanest small-cap reads on AI cluster build-out — every GPU/XPU and 1.6Tb optical module needs precision timing. A Barclays double-upgrade to Overweight with a PT lifted to $850 (from $400) layered onto the print, sending shares +24% intraday May 7. Read-throughs to other AI infrastructure picks: AVGO, MRVL, COHR.

image.alt: SiTime Q1 2026 earnings press release cover

items.title: agilon health Q1 2026 — adj EBITDA $54M (vs $20.6M Y/Y), EPS $1.80 trounces $1.04 est; raises FY26 EBITDA to $10–$40M, AGL +28%
items.summary: Value-based care platform agilon swung to $48.9M net income on $1.42B revenue (-7% Y/Y); adj EBITDA jumped to $53.8M from $20.6M. Management lifted FY26 adj EBITDA midpoint to $25M (range $10–$40M, vs prior -$15 to $15M). AGL +27.9%.

explainer.tagline: agilon's data overhaul lands — Q1 EBITDA more than doubles, FY guide gets a sharp raise, and AGL pops on a bullish CEO handoff.
explainer.whatIsIt: agilon health is a value-based care platform that partners with primary care physicians on Medicare Advantage and ACO REACH risk-bearing contracts. The company reported Q1 2026 results on May 6, 2026 — the same day Tim O'Rourke took over as CEO.
explainer.howItWorks: Revenue fell 7% Y/Y to $1.42B as MA membership dropped to 426K from 491K (a deliberate book pruning). But profitability surged: net income climbed to $48.9M from $12.1M, adjusted EBITDA jumped to $53.8M from $20.6M, and adjusted EPS of $1.80 crushed the $1.04 consensus. Management lifted FY26 adjusted EBITDA guidance to a $10–$40M range from prior $(15)–$15M; FY26 revenue guide is $5.68–$5.81B; medical margin guide $350–$400M. Enhanced data pipelines now provide validated claims/risk scores on ~85% of members, with the new CHF pathway shifting heart-failure diagnosis earlier in the care cycle (inpatient first-diagnosis rate from ~25% to <5%).
explainer.whyItMatters: agilon spent 2025 in turnaround mode after a series of guide cuts; this is the first quarter where the data/risk-management investments show up in the numbers. The +27.9% move is the biggest single-day re-rating since 2024, and the read-through hits other risk-bearing managed-care names (HUM, OSCR, CLOV).

items.title: Airbnb Q1 2026 — revenue $2.7B (+18%) beats but EPS $0.26 misses $0.30; raises FY26 to low-mid teens growth despite Iran-driven cancellations in EMEA/APAC
items.summary: Airbnb posted Q1 2026 revenue of $2.7B (+18%), adj EBITDA $519M (+24%), and free cash flow $1.7B. EPS of $0.26 missed the $0.30 consensus and ABNB slipped ~2% AH as Mideast cancellations bite, but FY26 guide goes up.

explainer.tagline: Travel demand stayed strong in Q1 but Mideast bookings softened, leaving ABNB stuck between a beat on revenue and a miss on EPS.
explainer.whatIsIt: Airbnb posted Q1 2026 results after the close on May 7, 2026. Revenue grew 18% to $2.7B, gross booking value rose 19% to $29.2B, and the company guided FY26 revenue growth to a low-to-mid teens range — up from a prior 12% outlook.
explainer.howItWorks: Net income came in at $160M (6% margin); adjusted EBITDA was $519M, +24% Y/Y at a 19% margin. Free cash flow hit $1.7B for the quarter. EPS of $0.26 missed the $0.30 LSEG consensus. Q2 revenue guide of $3.54–$3.60B is above Street and FY26 adjusted EBITDA margin is now expected to be at least 35%.
explainer.whyItMatters: Airbnb flagged 'slightly elevated' cancellations in EMEA and APAC tied to the Middle East conflict — a direct read-through to other travel names. The raised FY26 guide and the FCF print give cover to repurchases, but the EPS miss and Mideast drag are why the stock fell ~2% AH.

items.title: CoreWeave Q1 2026 — revenue $2.08B (2x Y/Y) beats $1.97B est, $99.4B backlog, NVIDIA buys $2B equity stake; CRWV ~-10% AH on capex creep
items.summary: CoreWeave doubled revenue to $2.08B and ended Q1 with a $99.4B backlog and 3.5GW of contracted power. NVIDIA committed a $2B equity investment and CRWV closed an $8.5B DDTL term loan, but shares fell ~10% AH as 2026 capex moved up to $31–35B.

explainer.tagline: Revenue more than doubled and the backlog hit $99B, but capex creeping up and a soft Q2 framing knocked CRWV ~10% after hours.
explainer.whatIsIt: CoreWeave reported its first quarter as a public company that lapped a comparable Q1 — revenue of $2.08B versus $982M a year ago. The bigger headlines: a $2B equity investment from NVIDIA, an $8.5B non-recourse DDTL 4.0 facility, and a contracted power footprint above 3.5 GW with active power crossing 1 GW.
explainer.howItWorks: GAAP net loss widened to $740M ($1.40 per share) from $315M as financing costs and depreciation on the new fleet ramped. The $99.4B revenue backlog, up sharply Q/Q, signals multi-year visibility. Management raised the high end of 2026 capex guidance to $31–$35B from $30–$35B.
explainer.whyItMatters: CRWV is the cleanest pure-play on AI compute capex — backlog growth and the NVIDIA equity tie show demand intact, but the higher capex and lack of a strong Q2 revenue print left holders looking at deeper losses for longer. Read-throughs: $NVDA, $AMD, $VRT, $ETN.

items.title: Rocket Lab Q1 2026 — record $200.3M revenue (+63.5%), GAAP gross margin 38.2%, $2.2B backlog; Q2 guide $225–240M after Mynaric close + Motiv deal
items.summary: Rocket Lab beat all Q1 2026 guidance metrics: $200.3M revenue (+63.5%), record GAAP gross margin 38.2%, adjusted EBITDA loss narrowed to $11.8M, and backlog hit $2.2B. Q2 revenue guide of $225–240M sets up another record. Mynaric closed; Motiv agreement signed.

explainer.tagline: Rocket Lab beat every guidance metric this quarter, signed a Mars-robotics deal, and pre-printed another record in Q2.
explainer.whatIsIt: Rocket Lab's Q1 2026 results posted record revenue of $200.3M, a 63.5% Y/Y jump, with GAAP gross margin of 38.2% and non-GAAP gross margin of 43.0% — both the highest the company has reported.
explainer.howItWorks: Adjusted EBITDA loss narrowed to $(11.8)M from $(30.0)M as scale leveraged across Electron, Space Systems, and Neutron-related work. Backlog grew 20% Q/Q to $2.2B; total launch manifest now exceeds 70 contracted missions. Rocket Lab guided Q2 revenue to $225–$240M with non-GAAP gross margin of 38–40%.
explainer.whyItMatters: The Mynaric close gives Rocket Lab a European laser-comms footprint for sat constellations, and the Motiv Space Systems agreement adds Mars-proven robotics. With the Neutron program advancing and government work building, RKLB extended its lead among small/medium-launch listed names while SpaceX stays private.

items.title: Cloudflare Q1 2026 — revenue $639.8M (+34%), non-GAAP NI $94M; announces ~1,100-employee layoff (~20%) in 'agentic AI-first' reorg, $140–150M charge
items.summary: Cloudflare posted Q1 2026 revenue of $639.8M (+34%), non-GAAP net income $94M, and FCF $84.1M. Alongside results it announced a ~1,100-employee (~20%) workforce cut to pivot to an 'agentic AI-first' operating model, taking a $140–150M restructuring charge.

explainer.tagline: Cloudflare beat the print and raised the FY guide, then announced a 20% headcount cut to retool around 'agentic AI-first' workflows.
explainer.whatIsIt: Cloudflare reported Q1 2026 revenue of $639.8M (+34% Y/Y), non-GAAP net income of $94M, and free cash flow of $84.1M. The FY26 revenue guide moved up to $2,805–$2,813M with non-GAAP EPS of $1.19–$1.20.
explainer.howItWorks: GAAP loss from operations was $62M; non-GAAP operating income rose to $73.1M from $56M Y/Y. Alongside results, Cloudflare disclosed a workforce reduction of about 1,100 employees (~20% of headcount), with $140–$150M in charges — $105–$110M in cash for severance and notice and $35–$40M in stock-based vesting. Most of the charges are expected in Q2–Q3 2026.
explainer.whyItMatters: A 20% AI-driven headcount cut at a profitable, high-growth software vendor is the clearest signal yet that 'agentic AI' is moving from a margin tailwind to an org redesign. Watch read-throughs to other developer-platform names (DDOG, NET, FSLY, ESTC) and to the broader hiring/SaaS labor mix.

items.title: Expedia Q1 2026 — revenue $3.43B (+15%), adj EPS $1.96 trounces $1.25 est, new $5B buyback; EXPE ~-7% AH on soft Q2 guide ($4.11–4.19B)
items.summary: Expedia posted Q1 2026 revenue of $3.43B (+15%), adjusted EPS of $1.96 versus $1.25 expected, gross bookings +13% with B2B +22%. The board added a new $5B buyback authorization, but EXPE fell ~7% AH on a Q2 revenue guide ($4.11–$4.19B) light of consensus.

explainer.tagline: Expedia delivered the highest Q1 profitability in its history and a new $5B buyback, but a soft Q2 revenue guide left holders unconvinced.
explainer.whatIsIt: Expedia Group reported Q1 2026 results after the close on May 7, 2026. Revenue grew 15% to $3.43B and adjusted EPS came in at $1.96 versus a $1.25 consensus — the highest Q1 profitability in the company's history per management.
explainer.howItWorks: Total gross bookings rose 13%, with B2B up 22% and B2C up 10%. GAAP net loss narrowed 97% Y/Y while adjusted EBITDA grew 83%. Expedia repurchased $700M of stock (3.3M shares) in the quarter and authorized a new $5B buyback. The Q2 revenue guide of $4.11–$4.19B is below Street, and full-year guidance was reaffirmed rather than raised.
explainer.whyItMatters: Travel demand at the print looked solid, but the Q2 framing — same FY guide, soft Q2 — is the read-through. Pair this with Airbnb's Mideast cancellation commentary (also May 7) and a wobble in OTA / leisure-travel sentiment is harder to brush aside. Watch ABNB, BKNG and the major airline names into Q2.

items.title: Vistra Q1 2026 — net income $1.03B vs ($268M) loss prior year, EBITDA $1.49B; reaffirms FY26 guide while Cogentrix close and Meta nuclear PPA still ahead
items.summary: Vistra swung to a $1.03B Q1 profit on $1.49B Adjusted EBITDA, reaffirmed FY26 guidance of $6.8–$7.6B EBITDA, and pointed to the pending 5,500-MW Cogentrix close and Meta PJM nuclear PPAs as upside.

explainer.tagline: Vistra prints $1.03B Q1 profit and points to Cogentrix + Meta nuclear PPAs as the next legs of the AI-power story.
explainer.whatIsIt: Vistra Corp., the largest U.S. independent power producer with the country's second-biggest nuclear fleet, reported Q1 2026 results before the bell on May 7. The company swung from a Q1 2025 GAAP net loss of $268M to net income of $1,029M, helped by a $723M unrealized hedge gain.
explainer.howItWorks: Operating revenues rose to $5.64B from $3.93B a year earlier and Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA reached $1,494M (vs $1,240M). Management reaffirmed full-year 2026 Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA guidance of $6.8–$7.6B and Adjusted FCFbG guidance of $3.925–$4.725B. The $1.5B remaining buyback authorization stays in place after roughly $6.3B repurchased since November 2021, cutting share count ~30%.
explainer.whyItMatters: The reaffirmed guide explicitly excludes the 5,500-MW Cogentrix natural-gas portfolio, which Vistra still expects to close in H2 2026, and the recently signed long-term Meta power purchase agreements at PJM nuclear sites that begin contributing to EBITDA in 2027. With Fitch joining S&P at investment grade, Vistra is positioning itself as the lowest-cost option for hyperscalers signing 24/7 clean-power contracts. Stock fell ~1.8% on the day as the print included no headline guide raise.
explainer.forWho: AI-power and IPP traders, utilities desks

items.title: Trade Desk Q1 2026 — revenue $689M (+12%) beats but adj EPS $0.28 misses $0.32; Q2 guide above Street, stock plunges ~14% AH
items.summary: Trade Desk beat on revenue at $689M (+12%) but adj EPS of $0.28 missed $0.32 estimates and adj EBITDA was flat at $206M; Q2 guide of $750M+ revenue and $260M EBITDA fell short of the bull case and TTD plunged ~14% AH.

explainer.tagline: Top-line beat and a $750M+ Q2 guide weren't enough — Trade Desk shares cratered ~14% AH on EPS miss and shrinking margins.
explainer.whatIsIt: The Trade Desk, the largest independent demand-side advertising platform, reported Q1 2026 results after the bell on May 7. The print was a textbook beat-and-miss: revenue of $689M beat the $679M consensus but non-GAAP EPS of $0.28 missed by 4 cents, and adjusted EBITDA was flat at $206M.
explainer.howItWorks: Revenue grew 12% Y/Y, a sharp deceleration from the prior year's 25%. Adjusted EBITDA margin contracted from 34% to 30% and GAAP net income fell to $40M from $51M. Q2 guidance called for revenue of at least $750M and adjusted EBITDA of approximately $260M. Customer retention stayed above 95% and TTD repurchased $164M of stock during the quarter, leaving $327M authorized.
explainer.whyItMatters: The 14% AH plunge takes TTD back near its 52-week lows and signals investors are no longer willing to pay a premium for sub-15% growth. The miss raises questions about how Kokai (the AI-powered platform overhaul rolled out last year) is actually scaling, and whether agency-tier ad budgets are reallocating to walled gardens (Amazon, YouTube). Comp tickers PUBM, MGNI and ad-name DV likely take a sympathy hit on the open.

items.title: Coinbase Q1 2026 — net loss $394M and adj EBITDA $303M; trading-volume share hits ATH 8.6%, derivatives revenue tops $200M annualized, prediction markets at $100M ARR
items.summary: Coinbase posted Q1 net loss of $394M and Adjusted EBITDA of $303M, but flagged a record 8.6% global crypto trading-volume market share, $200M+ annualized retail derivatives revenue and prediction markets ramping to $100M ARR in two months.

explainer.tagline: Net loss but record trading-volume share — and derivatives + prediction markets are scaling faster than the legacy spot business is shrinking.
explainer.whatIsIt: Coinbase Global, the largest U.S.-listed crypto exchange, reported Q1 2026 results after the bell on May 7. Q1 was a tough macro backdrop — BTC fell 22% in the quarter and ETH dropped 41%, hitting trading volumes industry-wide — but Coinbase took share and showed traction in adjacent product lines.
explainer.howItWorks: GAAP net loss came in at $394.1M with Adjusted EBITDA of $303.3M. Global crypto trading-volume market share hit a new all-time-high of 8.6%, trailing-twelve-month derivatives volume grew 169% Y/Y, and retail derivatives are now generating over $200M of annualized revenue. Prediction markets — barely two months live — are at over $100M annualized run-rate. Coinbase carries roughly $19B of average USDC on-platform (>25% of total USDC supply), and Base settled 62% of all global onchain stablecoin transaction volume in the quarter.
explainer.whyItMatters: The print lands two days after CEO Brian Armstrong cut 700 jobs (14% of headcount), citing cost growth that outran the bull market. Net loss is dominated by non-cash markdowns on Coinbase's crypto holdings and Circle stake, not core ops, so the bull thesis hinges on whether derivatives + stablecoins + prediction markets can offset a structurally smaller spot-trading business. COIN is a key barometer for crypto-equity proxies and ETH-Layer-2 names alike.

items.title: Microchip Q4 FY26 — revenue $1.311B (+35% Y/Y, +11% Q/Q) tops guide, non-GAAP EPS $0.57 vs $0.48–$0.52 guide; Q1 FY27 revenue guide $1.456B as AI/data-center design wins build
items.summary: Microchip's FQ4 print blew through the high end of guidance — revenue $1.311B (+35% Y/Y), non-GAAP EPS $0.57, gross margin 61.6% — and the FQ1 FY27 guide of $1.456B (+11% Q/Q) added to the analog-recovery + AI-datacenter narrative.

explainer.tagline: Microchip blows past the high end of guide and signals another sequential step-up — analog/MCU cycle has clearly turned.
explainer.whatIsIt: Microchip Technology, the analog/microcontroller and FPGA supplier, reported its FQ4 (March 2026 quarter) and full-year results after the bell on May 7. Both top and bottom lines came in well above the company's own guidance, capping a steep recovery from the late-2024 inventory trough.
explainer.howItWorks: Q4 revenue of $1.311B beat the $1.27B Street estimate and topped Microchip's own $1.26B (+/- $20M) guide midpoint, growing 35.1% Y/Y and 10.6% sequentially. Non-GAAP EPS of $0.57 cleared the $0.48–$0.52 guide range; non-GAAP gross margin reached 61.6%. Management guided FQ1 FY27 to revenue of $1.456B at the midpoint (+35.3% Y/Y, +11.0% Q/Q) and non-GAAP EPS of $0.67–$0.71. CEO Steve Sanghi flagged 'broad-based improvement' and continued progress on the nine-point recovery plan.
explainer.whyItMatters: MCHP's print is one of the cleanest data points yet that the broad analog/MCU cycle has turned, with management explicitly calling out 'strong customer engagement and expanding design activity in data center and AI applications' driven by Gen6 PCIe retimer wins. Reads through to ADI, ON, TXN and STM, and supports the narrative that the AI capex wave is now broadening beyond GPUs into power-management, optics, retimers and connectivity silicon.

image.alt: Microchip Technology Q4 FY2026 financial results announcement

items.title: SoundHound AI Q1 2026 — record $44.2M revenue (+52%, +88% ex-acquisitions in core auto/IoT); launches OASYS agentic-AI platform, reaffirms 2026 guide $225–260M
items.summary: SoundHound AI posted record Q1 revenue of $44.2M (+52% Y/Y, +88% ex-acquisitions in core auto/IoT), launched its 'OASYS' agentic-AI platform, and reaffirmed FY26 guide of $225–260M with a 2027 outlook of $350–400M including LivePerson.

explainer.tagline: Record Q1 print, agentic-AI platform launch, and a 2027 guide that bakes in the pending LivePerson deal — SoundHound is hunting Fortune 100 enterprise budgets.
explainer.whatIsIt: SoundHound AI, the conversational/voice-AI vendor, reported Q1 2026 results after the bell on May 7. The company also launched OASYS, which it pitches as a self-learning orchestrated agentic-AI platform that can spin up, evaluate and improve agents autonomously.
explainer.howItWorks: Q1 revenue of $44.2M was up 52% Y/Y (a Q1 record) and the core automotive/IoT vertical grew 88% Y/Y excluding the impact of acquisitions. GAAP net loss was $25.0M; gross margin was 31.1% GAAP / 49.7% non-GAAP. The company exited Q1 with $216M of cash and no debt. FY26 revenue guidance of $225–$260M was reaffirmed; the 2027 outlook of $350–$400M is contingent on closing the previously announced LivePerson acquisition (all-stock, ~$43M equity / ~$250M EV, expected H2 2026).
explainer.whyItMatters: SoundHound's Q1 puts a number on its claim of 88% organic growth in the AI vertical that's hardest to fake (in-vehicle voice). Combined with the OASYS launch, the company is making a play to compete with the big-vendor agentic-AI stacks (Salesforce, ServiceNow, Microsoft Copilot) in customer-experience workflows. The reaffirmed guide gives bulls a clean target; the LivePerson close gives bears something to pick at.

image.alt: SoundHound AI Q1 2026 results announcement

items.title: Datadog Q1 2026 — revenue $1.006B (+32%) crosses $1B for the first time, non-GAAP EPS $0.60 vs $0.51 est, raises FY26 guide; DDOG ~+28% intraday
items.summary: Datadog Q1 revenue $1.006B grows 32% Y/Y, first $1B quarter; non-GAAP EPS $0.60 beats $0.51 est, FCF $289M, $100k+ ARR customers +21% Y/Y; raises FY26 revenue guide to $4.30–$4.34B. DDOG ~+28%.

explainer.tagline: Datadog crosses $1B in quarterly revenue for the first time as AI workloads keep flowing into observability.
explainer.whatIsIt: Datadog reported Q1 2026 results on May 7. Revenue and earnings beat consensus and the company raised its full-year outlook, with management citing AI-driven workload growth and faster expansion among its largest customers.
explainer.howItWorks: Revenue was $1,006M, up 32% Y/Y — the first $1B+ quarter. Non-GAAP EPS was $0.60 vs $0.51 est. Non-GAAP operating income hit $223M (22% margin); GAAP operating income was $7M (1% margin). Free cash flow was $289M. $100k+ ARR customers grew to ~4,550 from ~3,770 a year earlier. FY26 guide was lifted to $4.30–$4.34B revenue (25–27% growth) and $2.36–$2.44 non-GAAP EPS. Q2 revenue guide is $1.07–$1.08B, above Street.
explainer.whyItMatters: Shares ran roughly 28% on the day, the largest single-session move in years, on confirmation that AI inference and agent workloads are translating into higher cloud-monitoring spend. The print reads through positively to infra-software peers (Dynatrace, MongoDB, Snowflake) and reinforces the AI-capex-meets-software thesis after AMD's Q1 beat earlier in the week.
explainer.forWho: software/infra equity desks, AI capex bulls

items.title: HawkEye 360 (HAWK) NYSE debut — prices $416M IPO at $26 top of range, opens $33.92 (~+30%) at ~$2.4B implied valuation
items.summary: Space-based RF intelligence firm HawkEye 360 prices 16M shares at $26 (top of $24–$26 range), raises $416M, opens +30% at $33.92 on NYSE debut at ~$2.4B valuation; Goldman/Morgan Stanley led.

explainer.tagline: Space-based RF intelligence firm HawkEye 360 lands on the NYSE at $33.92, ~+30% above its $26 IPO price.
explainer.whatIsIt: HawkEye 360 began trading under the ticker HAWK on the NYSE on May 7. The deal priced on May 6 at $26 — top of the marketed $24–$26 range — placing 16 million shares for $416M in gross proceeds. Underwriters hold a 30-day overallotment for an additional 2.4M shares.
explainer.howItWorks: Shares opened at $33.92, roughly 30% above the IPO price. The deal valued the company at ~$2.4B at the issue price. Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley led as joint book-running managers, with RBC Capital Markets, Jefferies, and BofA Securities as additional bookrunners. The company operates a constellation of small satellites that detects, geolocates, and characterizes radio-frequency emissions for U.S. and allied defense, intelligence, and national security customers.

A clean +30% open through Goldman/Morgan Stanley validates investor appetite for primary defense-tech paper and reads positively for Seraphim Space Investment Trust (LON: SSIT), a pre-IPO holder.

items.title: Rackspace Q1 2026 + AMD AI pact — revenue $678M beats, swings to $8M GAAP profit; AMD MOU on governed AI infrastructure sends RXT ~+88% intraday
items.summary: Rackspace beat on Q1 revenue ($678M, +2%) and swung to an $8M GAAP profit, but the real catalyst is a multi-year MOU with AMD to build a governed enterprise AI cloud on Instinct GPUs and EPYC CPUs.

explainer.tagline: Rackspace prints a clean Q1 beat and pairs it with an AMD MOU to build a governed, fully managed AI cloud — RXT roughly doubles intraday.
explainer.whatIsIt: Rackspace Technology released Q1 2026 results pre-market on May 7 and simultaneously announced a non-binding multi-year MOU with AMD. The deal positions Rackspace to operate a fully managed enterprise AI stack — from AMD Instinct GPUs and EPYC CPUs up through inference runtime and SLA — pitched at regulated and sovereign workloads. RXT shares spiked about 88% intraday to roughly $4.28.
explainer.howItWorks: Q1 revenue was $678M (+2% reported, +1% constant currency), narrowly above the $675M consensus. Public cloud grew 7% to $443M while private cloud declined 6% to $235M. Rackspace swung to $8M of net income and $0.03 GAAP EPS, with adjusted loss of ($0.06). Full-year 2026 guidance is for $2.6–2.7B in revenue and an adjusted loss of ($0.20)–($0.15) per share. The AMD pact — outlined as Enterprise AI Cloud, Enterprise Inference Engine, Inference-as-a-Service and bare-metal Instinct compute — is non-binding but moves Rackspace from hourly GPU rental into a fully governed managed stack.
explainer.whyItMatters: Rackspace is leveraging the only piece of moat it still has (multi-cloud managed services) to ride the AI infrastructure capex cycle. The AMD partnership is a credibility marker for AMD's Instinct roadmap and a re-rating catalyst for RXT, which has spent three years de-rating on private-cloud declines. Watch comp names AMD, NVDA, ORCL (sovereign-cloud overlap), and AI-managed-services peers like CRWV and IRDM.
explainer.forWho: AI-infrastructure investors, cloud sector analysts, AMD followers

items.title: Fluence Energy FQ2 2026 — revenue $464.9M misses big but record $5.6B backlog and two hyperscaler MSAs send FLNC ~+20–27% AH
items.summary: Fluence Energy revenue +7.7% to $464.9M missed the $635.5M consensus, but a record $5.6B backlog, doubled YTD order intake and master supply agreements with two hyperscalers drove FLNC up sharply after-hours.

explainer.tagline: Fluence's revenue line missed by ~$170M but the backlog hit a record $5.6B and two hyperscaler MSAs validated the data-center pivot.
explainer.whatIsIt: Battery-storage integrator Fluence Energy reported FQ2 fiscal 2026 results after the close on May 6. The headline revenue print fell well short of consensus, but the company posted a record backlog, doubled year-to-date order intake, and signed master supply agreements with two unnamed hyperscaler customers. Shares surged 20–27% in after-hours trading.
explainer.howItWorks: Q2 revenue of $464.9M was up 7.7% Y/Y but missed the $635.5M Street estimate. Net loss narrowed to $29.2M from $41.9M; GAAP gross margin expanded to 10.0% and adjusted gross margin to 11.1%. Adjusted EBITDA was ($9.4M), an improvement on the prior year. Backlog reached $5.6B at March 31 — a company record — while year-to-date order intake doubled to about $2.0B. Total liquidity stood at roughly $900M including $412.9M of cash. The company reaffirmed FY26 guidance for revenue of $3.2–3.6B, adjusted EBITDA of $40–60M, and ARR of about $180M by year-end.
explainer.whyItMatters: The print resets the narrative for FLNC: forward demand has clearly inflected even as quarterly revenue is lumpy. The hyperscaler MSAs are the first tangible link between data-center capex and grid-scale storage, and the read-through is positive for storage peers TSLA Energy, STEM, and EOSE. The reaffirmed full-year guide combined with the $5.6B backlog makes the FY27 setup easier to underwrite.
explainer.forWho: energy-transition funds, growth-equity investors, AI-power-thesis traders
